videos.title: Over 50? The Best Time to Drink Beet Juice (5 Time Windows Explained) | Senior Health

transcript.fullText: Did you know that beet juice has been used for centuries as a natural remedy? It contains many compounds that can energize your body, boost energy levels, and even improve your overall health. Beets are a true nutritional powerhouse. And when they are juiced, they become a vibrant drink with remarkable potential for your health. But here is the surprising part. There is a question that is even more important than the recipe. When you drink it, timing can determine whether you feel good or feel unwell. to fully benefit from its nutrients. Today, I will rank five time windows during the day, from the ones you should avoid to the most optimal, so you know which time windows are the safest and which ones are more likely to trigger unwanted reactions. This applies to three common groups among people over 50. After that, there is a very simple 7-day plan so you can test it yourself step by step in a safe and clear way. All references are listed in the description below. Now I will go straight to the most important part. Now I will go straight to the most important part. I am going to rank five time windows for drinking beet juice from the worst to the best. Specifically for people over 50, especially if you care about blood sugar stability and how steady you feel after drinking it. And I want to repeat one simple principle. Not every time window works for everyone, but there are certain times when the body is more likely to react. and other times that are generally safer. Time window number five, the worst time. The worst time, which I rank at number five, is drinking beet juice immediately after waking up on a completely empty stomach. Many people choose this time because they believe drinking it in the morning is the healthiest option. But for people over 50, this is often the most uncomfortable time if you are sensitive to blood pressure or blood sugar. When the stomach is empty, absorption tends to happen faster. And when the response comes quickly, the body feels it more clearly. If nitric oxide production is strong, some people may feel lightaded or slightly dizzy. If you are taking blood pressure medication, this sensation can sometimes be more noticeable. When it comes to blood sugar, the issue is often not just whether the juice tastes sweet, but how quickly sugar enters the bloodstream. If you drink it quickly or mix it with sweet fruit, sugar can rise faster, making you feel shaky, drained, or fatigued. So, if you have ever drunk beet juice in the morning and felt unwell afterward, do not rush to conclude that beet juice is not right for you. Very often, it is simply a sign that you need to change the timing. Time window number four, a high-risk time. The next risky time, which I rank at number four, is drinking beet juice late at night, close to bedtime. This time has two main issues. First, the body is preparing to enter a resting rhythm. So, anything that changes circulation sensations or causes digestive discomfort can interfere with sleep. Second, many people over 50 already wake up easily during the night. So, drinking close to bedtime, especially in larger amounts, can lead to bloating or increased nighttime urination, disrupting sleep. And if your goal is cardiovascular and circulation health, sleep is something you cannot afford to neglect. So, if drinking beet juice affects your sleep, that is simply not a worthwhile trade-off. Next, I will talk about time window number three, which is a neutral option and easier to maintain as a habit. After that, I will move on to the two best time windows where most people over 50 tend to feel safer, especially when it comes to blood sugar stability. Time window number three. Now, I continue with time window number three. This is a neutral time window, meaning it is not the best, but it is easy to maintain and usually causes little trouble if you need a stable routine. This time window typically falls in the midm morning or early afternoon when the body is already awake. The stomach is no longer completely empty like right after waking up and the body has not yet shifted into a resting rhythm like in the evening. For many people over 50, this is a less sensitive period. So the sensation after drinking is often more comfortable than drinking on a fully empty stomach. If you tend to forget, stay busy, or simply want to choose a fixed time window to observe your body over a few days, this is a reasonable option. However, because it is neutral, you may not always feel a very clear effect compared with times that are better matched with context, such as drinking around meals or light movement. Time window number two. Now we move to time window number two where most people over 50 begin to notice a clearer difference especially if they previously experienced discomfort. Time window number two is drinking beet juice after a meal about 30 to 60 minutes later. This is a time when the body usually tolerates it more safely because food in the stomach slows absorption and reduces the feeling that everything arrives too quickly. The same applies to blood sugar. Drinking after a meal is usually steadier than drinking on an empty stomach, especially if the meal contains enough protein and fiber. If you have ever drunk beet juice and felt shaky, low on energy, or inexplicably tired, simply switching to drinking it after a meal is enough for many people to feel a difference. Another important point is that if your stomach is sensitive, drinking after a meal also reduces irritation and discomfort. Time window number one. Finally, we come to time window number one. The best option for people over 50 when your goal is circulation while still keeping the body safe and stable, especially if you want to avoid large blood sugar swings. Time window number one is drinking beet juice before light activity about 60 to 120 minutes before you go for a walk. The reason is very simple. When beet juice supports vasoddilation signals and you then activate the body with light movement such as walking, the circulation effect tends to be better directed and many people feel their legs more comfortable, warmer, and lighter with each step. At the same time, light movement helps the body handle energy more efficiently. So, the sensation after drinking is usually more stable than drinking and then sitting still. The key point is that you do not need intense exercise. A walk of 15 to 25 minutes is enough to test how your body responds. And this is the point many people overlook. They drink beet juice and then remain sedentary or they drink it on a completely empty stomach. So they only feel the reaction and not the benefit. When you choose the right timing and the right context, you give your body a chance to receive the benefits instead of just experiencing discomfort. Now that you understand the best times of day to drink beet juice, next I will clearly explain how to apply this in real life for the three most common groups. Those concerned about blood sugar, those who tend to experience low blood pressure or are taking blood pressure medication, and those with sensitive digestion or who are prone to poor sleep. Now, I'll help you apply this correctly based on your group. Now, I'll help you apply this correctly based on your own group because this is where many people get it wrong. They hear one general piece of advice and apply it exactly the same way even though each body over 50 has a different level of sensitivity. You just need to place yourself into one of the three groups below and follow the matching time window. Group one, concerned about blood sugar. The first group is people who worry about blood sugar or who have felt shaky, low on energy, or unusually tired after drinking beet juice, especially if you previously drank beet juice mixed with a lot of sweet fruits. For this group, the most important rule is to avoid drinking on an empty stomach. The most suitable time window is usually 30 to 60 minutes after a meal. If you want even more stability, start with a small amount and dilute it. Your goal is not to drink it strong, but to drink it safely. When you drink after a meal, absorption tends to be slower, reactions are steadier, and your body usually feels more comfortable. If you want to try time window number one, before walking, you can do so, but only after your body has already adjusted to beet juice for a few days, and you should still avoid drinking on a completely empty stomach. One more important detail is not to drink it too quickly. Drinking fast makes everything arrive too fast. Drinking slowly over a few minutes helps the body accept it more gently. Group two, low blood pressure or blood pressure medication. The second group includes people who tend to have low blood pressure, feel dizzy when standing up, or are taking blood pressure or heart medications. For this group, a common mistake is drinking beet juice first thing in the morning right after waking up because it seems healthy. In reality, if your body is already sensitive, drinking on an empty stomach can make you feel laded more easily. For this group, the safest time window is usually still after a meal, and you need to pay close attention to the amount. You do not need to drink a lot to get benefits. You only need enough for your body to tolerate it. If after drinking you feel lightaded, unsteady, or unsure on your feet, that is a signal to reduce the amount and change the timing. If you want to try drinking before walking, do it very gently. Drink a smaller and more diluted amount. Walk slowly and always prioritize safety. Your goal is not to prove that you can tolerate it, but to find a way that you can maintain long term. Group three, sensitive stomach or poor sleep. The third group includes people with sensitive stomachs who easily feel discomfort or irritation and those who have trouble sleeping or wake up frequently at night. For this group, the two things to avoid most are drinking on an empty stomach and drinking close to bedtime. A sensitive stomach does not like things entering too quickly without a food base. And sleep does not like things that make you feel full or cause you to wake up repeatedly. Because of that, the most suitable time window for this group is usually after lunch or in the early afternoon when you have food in your system and are still far from bedtime. If you feel fine and want to try drinking before walking, you can, but choose early afternoon, not late evening. The goal is for your body to feel light without stomach pressure and for your sleep to remain unaffected. Summary. In short, if you are concerned about blood sugar, prioritize drinking after meals. If you are sensitive to blood pressure or are taking medication, you should even more strongly prioritize drinking after meals and using a small amount. If you have a sensitive stomach or poor sleep, absolutely avoid drinking on an empty stomach and close to bedtime and choose after lunch as the safest option. What comes next? And now I will give you a very simple 7-day plan so you can test this step by step in a safe and clear way. Now, this is the part you should stay until the end because this is the simplest way to find the right time window for your body without guessing and without pushing the dose too far. This plan is designed for people over 50 and its goal is to help your body adjust step by step so you can observe your reactions clearly day one and day two. Choose the safest time window for most people over 50, which is drinking beet juice 30 to 60 minutes after a meal. Start with a small amount and dilute it. Do not try to make it concentrated and do not try to drink a large amount in the first two days. The only goal is that your body accepts it smoothly without stomach irritation, without lightadedness, and while you can still go about your normal daily activities. You observe three simple things. First, whether your head feels clear and stable. Second, whether your stomach feels comfortable. Third, whether a few hours later you feel shaky or low on energy. If everything feels fine, keep it the same. Day three and day four, you still drink after a meal, but now you fix one specific time window and keep it the same each day. For example, always after lunch. The reason is that if you keep changing the timing, you cannot tell whether your body is reacting to the beet juice or to the timing itself. When you fix the time, the pattern becomes clearer. If you are in the blood sugar concern group, these two days are especially important because you will often feel how much smoother things are when you drink in the right context. If you are sensitive to blood pressure, these two days also help you safely check how much your body can tolerate. Day five. If the first four days feel fine, you can try time window number one, which is drinking before light activity about 60 to 120 minutes before walking. You still keep the amount small and diluted. Then you take a gentle walk for 15 to 25 minutes. The goal of day five is to see whether your legs feel lighter, circulation feels better, and your energy feels more stable. You do not need a strong feeling. You only need a feeling of being okay. If day five feels comfortable, that is a good sign. videos.title: Drink Beet Juice & 30 Minutes Later This Happens!

transcript.fullText: [Music] in a previous video we looked at the remarkable benefit beet juice has on lowering your blood pressure but a few comments raised concerns about the high sugar content of beet juice so let's take a look in this study researchers decided to look at beet juice's impact on blood sugar levels in the body they measured the post perennial glucose caused by either 225 milliliters of beet juice a control beverage matched for macronutrient content or a glucose beverage in 16 healthy adults postprandial blood sugar is a measurement of the glucose concentration blood sugar in your bloodstream in the period up to four hours after eating a meal postprandial finger prick blood samples were taken at 5 15 30 45 60 90 120 and 150 minutes over the course of the study to measure blood sugar levels in each group and researchers found that there was a significantly lower glucose response within the first 30 minutes in the beet juice group compared to the control group the researchers hypothesized that the major components found in beet juice such as betalains may account for the impact on blood sugar levels researchers of this study gave healthy participants 300 grams of carbohydrate in a meal with either 250 milliliters of beet juice or 250 milliliters of water and found that blood glucose levels at two hours post meal was significantly lowered in the test condition than in the control group the post treatment total cholesterol triglycerides and ldl values was significantly lower than the pre-treatment values in the test group in the control group there was no significant difference between pre and post-treatment in total cholesterol triglycerides hdl and ldl values the researchers concluded that quote beet juice should be incorporated as part of a daily diet and used as nutritional therapy as consumption of beet juice is beneficial for maintenance of good health they also said beetroot is rich in bioactive compounds that may provide health benefits with improved clinical outcomes for several pathologies such as hypertension atherosclerosis type 2 diabetes and dementia this meta-analysis highlighted that beet juice should be consumed raw as heat treatment may degrade the betalain content and finally in this small study obese participants who took beet juice with carbohydrates had reduced insulin resistance the non-obese participants which suggests that obese people may benefit from drinking beet juice and other nitrate-rich foods thank you so much for watching if you liked the video please give it a thumbs up leave a comment below and subscribe for more upcoming videos [Music] you

videos.title: Beet Juice For 12 Weeks! Here's What Happened!

transcript.fullText: [Music] an impressive new study led by the prestigious Imperial College London looked at the effect a daily beet juice supplement had on a devastating and irreversible chronic health condition that affects over 400 million people worldwide and in this video we're going to look at the impressive findings and highlight why this could be beneficial for everyone not just those suffering from this particular condition this study was one of the longest duration studies in this area so far and the results are quote very promising COPD is a serious and life-altering lung condition that currently has no cure the research has decided to see what impact a 12we course of a concentrated beetroot juice supplement that's high in nitrates had on this condition against a beet juice Placebo that looked and tasted the same but had the nitrate removed higher levels of nitrate in our blood can increase the availability of nitric oxide a chemical that helps our blood vessels relax it also increases the efficiency of our muscles meaning they need less oxygen to do the same work the study included 81 participants with COPD who are being treated at the raw Brompton Hospital in London and whose systolic blood pressure measured higher than 130 mm of mercury the scientist measured the participants blood pressure and tested how far patients could walk in 6 minutes at the beginning and end of the study participants were randomly allocated to either receive the 12we course of nitrate Rich beet supplement that was made up of 70 mL of concentrated beet juice this equated to 400 mg of nitrate once a day or the placebo and what the researchers found was extremely encouraging those taking the nitrate Rich beet juice supplement experienced a notable reduction in their systolic blood pressure compared to those who consumed the placebo and they walked 98 ft farther on average in 6 minutes the lead author said at the end of the study we found that the blood pressure of people taking the nitrate Rich beetroot juice drink was lower and their blood vessels became less stiff the juice also increased how far people with COPD could walk in 6 minutes compared to Placebo this is one of the longest duration studies in this area so far the results are very promising but will need to be confirmed in larger longer term studies now beet juice is not just beneficial for those with a chronic heart or lung condition but possibly for the majority of us beet juice has been growing in popularity here in the UK ever since Leicester city a football club in England overcame the Staggering odds of 5,000 to1 to win the Premier League title in 2016 the players in part credited it to beet juice which they drank every day scientists hypothesized that it helped them recover quicker than other players and help them to avoid injury during their stunning run to win the Premier League not only has Beach juice been shown to have an anti-inflammatory effect reduce muscle soreness after vigorous exercise but it also may increase stamina by improving oxygen utilization and time to exhaustion a word of caution beets are high in oxalates so if you're prone to calcium o oate kidney stones you may need to avoid beet juice and people with chronic kidney disease and endstage renal disease who follow a low pottassium diet need to consult their healthc care professional before consuming beet juice due to its high potassium content thank you so much for watching if you liked the video please give it a thumbs up leave a comment below and subscribe for more upcoming videos [Music] come [Music]

videos.title: Over 60? NEVER Drink Beet Juice With THESE 7 Foods - It Can Cause Serious Health Problems!

transcript.fullText: Did you know that one of the healthiest juices on the planet can become dangerous with just a single wrong ingredient? Many people start their morning feeling proud. They juice a fresh beet, pour it into a glass, and believe they're fueling their heart, cleansing their liver, and boosting their energy for the day. But what almost no one realizes is that beets are powerful. And like anything powerful, they require respect. Inside this humble red root are compounds that can transform your circulation, open your blood vessels, strengthen your liver, and supercharge stamina. Yet, those very compounds also react strongly when mixed with the wrong foods. And that's where silent trouble begins. Every day, thousands of people unknowingly create beet juice combinations that irritate the stomach, cause fermentation, raise oxalate levels, overload the liver, interfere with circulation, and even worse, cancel out the very nutrients they're trying to absorb. The scariest part, most of these harmful mixtures look healthy on the surface. Many are popular recipes printed in cookbooks, shared on social media, or recommended by friends who simply don't understand what's happening inside the body. But today, you will. In this video, we're going to uncover seven beet juice combinations you must never make, including one that can be genuinely dangerous for anyone over 50 or living with kidney or digestive issues. And more importantly, I'll show you exactly how to drink beet juice the right way so your heart, liver, circulation, and energy all benefit without risk. Stay with me to the end. What you learn today might completely change the way you prepare this healing juice forever. Combination number one, mixing beet juice with lemon. One of the most common mistakes people make is adding a big squeeze of lemon to their beet juice. It sounds healthy, right? Vitamin C, freshness, detox. What could possibly be wrong? Quite a lot, actually. Lemon is extremely acidic. And when that strong acidity collides with the natural compounds inside raw beetroot, the pH of the juice changes instantly. For many seniors, especially those with gastritis, acid reflux, or a sensitive stomach, this can trigger irritation within minutes. People often report heartburn, nausea, or a sour stomach shortly after drinking this combo without realizing the culprit is the lemon. But the bigger problem is what happens to beetroot's nutrients. Beets are naturally rich in plant-based iron and beneficial pigments that support blood building and circulation. Those nutrients require a stable internal environment to be absorbed properly. When lemon overly acidifies the mixture, it disrupts that process. Instead of enhancing absorption, the acidity actually interferes with it, especially iron absorption, cancelling one of the key benefits people expect from beet juice. What was supposed to support energy and oxygenation can end up giving you nothing but discomfort. There's also the issue of dizziness or weakness some people feel afterward. That's because the acidic shock can overstimulate stomach acid production, especially in older adults whose digestive lining is already thinner or more fragile. And if you're dealing with reflux, this combination can aggravate the esophagus further. Lemon itself isn't the villain. It's the quantity. A small splash for flavor can be harmless. But large squeezes, half a lemon, or the juice of a whole lemon, is where problems begin. If you enjoy brightness in your beet juice, use moderation. Just a few drops for freshness, never a heavy squeeze. And if your stomach is sensitive, skip the lemon entirely. Your body will thank you. Combination number two, mixing beet juice with honey or sugar. This is one of the most common mistakes people make and unfortunately it is also one of the most harmful. Many people don't enjoy the earthy taste of beetroot. So they try to fix it by adding honey, table sugar or even sweet syrups. It feels innocent. After all, honey is natural, right? But the moment you add concentrated sugars to beet juice, you completely change how the body processes it. Beets are naturally rich in nitrates that convert into nitric oxide, a compound that relaxes blood vessels, supports circulation, boosts stamina, and helps regulate blood pressure. But when you combine beet juice with honey or sugar, that nitrate conversion is disrupted. The result, the very benefit most people drink beet juice for improved blood flow becomes significantly weaker. What you're left with is a sugary drink that spikes blood glucose and offers only a fraction of the cardiovascular support beets are meant to deliver. For seniors, this sugar nitrate conflict is even more serious. Added sugars cause a rapid insulin response followed by inflammation and oxidative stress, especially in those with diabetes, pre-diabetes, liver issues, or metabolic syndrome. Honey, despite being natural, still behaves like a quick sugar in the bloodstream. This can accelerate fat buildup in the liver, worsen insulin resistance, and trigger energy crashes. And for those with high creatinine or kidney concerns, sugar overload forces the kidneys to work harder, the opposite of what your body needs. The good news, beetroot already has a mild natural sweetness when prepared correctly. Most people simply need time for their taste buds to adjust. But if you truly need to soften the flavor, choose low impact companions like green apple or cucumber. They add freshness, fiber, and hydration without interfering with nitrates or stressing your blood sugar. In short, sugar and beet juice don't belong together. If you want beet juice to act like a natural remedy, keep it pure. Your heart, liver, and energy levels will notice the difference. Combination number three, mixing beet juice with orange. On the surface, this combination sounds healthy. Two vitamin rich fruits blended into one refreshing drink. But for many people, especially seniors or anyone with digestive sensitivity, mixing beet juice with orange can quickly backfire. The issue begins with acidity. Oranges are highly acidic and when their acids meet the natural sugars and compounds in beetroot, the result is a recipe for intestinal fermentation. This means the mixture begins producing excess gas in the gut leading to bloating, cramping, and uncomfortable abdominal pressure. For people with gastritis, acid reflux, or IBS, this reaction can feel almost immediate. But digestion isn't the only concern. Oranges contain large amounts of vitamin C. And while vitamin C is essential, too much of it in a single drink can interfere with nitrate absorption. The very compounds in beetroot responsible for improving circulation and lowering blood pressure. When vitamin C dominates the blend, the nitrates convert less efficiently into nitric oxide, which reduces the cardiovascular benefits of beet juice dramatically. You think you're supporting your heart, but the combination quietly cancels out the effect. This mix also places extra demand on older digestive systems. The natural sugars in both beets and oranges digest quickly when juiced and without their original fiber structure. They rush into the bloodstream. This can spike blood glucose more suddenly than people expect, especially in seniors, those with pre-diabetes, or individuals with fluctuating blood pressure. If you enjoy citrus, it's far safer to consume beet juice and orange juice at separate times of the day, giving your stomach and circulatory system space to process each one properly. Beet juice works best on a relatively empty stomach, while orange juice is best taken earlier in the day with food to buffer its acidity. So, while the bright color and taste may look appealing, beet plus orange is one combination that does more harm than good, especially when consistency, digestion, and heart health matter most. Combination number four, mixing beet juice with vinegar. This may seem like a harmless blend. After all, vinegar shows up in detox drinks, homemade tonics, and countless health hacks. But when it comes to beet juice, pairing it with vinegar is one of the biggest mistakes you can make. Beetroot is naturally rich in nitrates, compounds that convert into nitric oxide and help open your blood vessels, improve circulation, and support healthy blood pressure. Vinegar, however, is extremely acidic, and that acidity can destroy a significant portion of these nitrates before your body even has a chance to use them. In other words, you lose the primary benefit that makes beet juice so valuable. But the problems go deeper than nutrient loss. The high acetic acid content in vinegar can irritate the lining of the stomach when combined with raw beet juice, especially for people who already deal with reflux, gastritis, or a sensitive digestive system. Many seniors describe a burning sensation, nausea, or a wave of stomach heaviness shortly after drinking this mix. And because both beetroot and vinegar influence blood pressure, beat by lowering it, vinegar by altering sodium and potassium balance, the combination can cause unexpected dips, dizziness, or weakness in people with naturally low blood pressure. For anyone trying to detox, adding vinegar may feel like the right move, but the truth is the opposite. Instead of helping the liver work more efficiently, the acidity makes the liver work harder to rebalance your internal pH. That means more metabolic stress, not less. And for people with kidney issues, this extra acid load becomes even more problematic, forcing the kidneys to neutralize compounds that the body should never have had to deal with in the first place. The best approach is simple. Keep beet juice pure. If you want a lighter, more refreshing texture, use water, cucumber, or a low acid fruit like green apple. Vinegar has its own benefits, just not when mixed with beetroot. Use it in salad dressings or cooking, but keep it far away from your morning beet juice if you want to protect your stomach, your blood pressure, and the very nutrients that make beetroot such a healing food. Combination number five, mixing beet juice with milk or dairy products. Some people try blending beet juice with milk to make it creamier, but this is one of the worst combinations you can make. On paper, it may seem harmless. Beets for nutrients, milk for protein. Yet inside your body, this mix triggers reactions that lead to digestive distress, nutrient loss, and inflammation. The issue begins with protein acid interaction. Beetroot is naturally acidic, and when beet juice comes into contact with milk's proteins, especially casein, it causes them to curdle prematurely. This curdling doesn't happen gently like yogurt fermentation. It happens suddenly inside your stomach. The result, heavy digestion, fermentation, and uncomfortable symptoms like bloating, gas, reflux, and a rock in the stomach feeling that can last for hours. But there's more. Milk requires a very specific digestive environment, while beetroot stimulates stomach acids and bile flow. When these two processes clash, your body struggles to break down both foods properly. That means you absorb less calcium from the milk and fewer vitamins from the beetroot. The exact opposite of what most people intend when they make this healthy smoothie. For people with lactose intolerance or slow digestion, which becomes more common after age 50, this combination often leads to nausea, cramping, or rapid onset of acidity. This pairing can also overload the liver. Beet juice increases nitric oxide activity and improves circulation, while dairy slows digestion and thickens stomach contents. When consumed together, the liver must work harder to filter metabolic byproducts from both foods at once, increasing inflammation instead of reducing it. For people with fatty liver, sluggish digestion, or a history of reflux, this combination should be avoided altogether. If you want a creamier beet drink, there are safer alternatives. Coconut water, for example, hydrates, supports electrolytes, and blends beautifully with beetroot without causing digestive conflict. Cucumber also adds smoothness and freshness while supporting kidney and liver cleansing. These pairings help your body work more efficiently, not harder. Dairy has its place, just not inside your beet juice. Keep them separate, and your stomach, liver, and energy levels will thank you. Combination number six, mixing beet juice with spinach. On their own, beets and spinach are nutritional powerhouses. But when you put them together in the same juice, especially in concentrated raw form, you accidentally create a biochemical problem your kidneys and bones don't appreciate. Both beets and spinach are extremely high in oxalates, natural plant compounds that bind to minerals like calcium, magnesium, and iron. When consumed in large amounts or paired together, oxalates can overwhelm your body's ability to process and eliminate them. The biggest risk, kidney stones. Oxalates from both foods join with calcium in the kidneys and form calcium oxilate crystals. The most common and most painful type of kidney stone for people with a history of stones, reduced kidney function, mild dehydration, or even low magnesium intake. This combination can quickly push the body into a high oxalate overload. You don't feel it immediately, but the crystals silently accumulate, increasing your risk week after week. Another hidden problem is mineral blocking. Oxalates grab onto important nutrients, especially calcium and iron, making them harder for your body to absorb. So, while many people combine beets and spinach thinking they're supercharging their juice, the reality is the opposite. You absorb less nutrition, and your digestive system has to work harder to eliminate what it can't use. Over time, this can weaken bones, worsen anemia, or contribute to fatigue and muscle weakness. People with gut issues such as IBS, gastritis, or low stomach acid are especially vulnerable. High oxilate juices pass through the digestive system too quickly, irritating the intestine and causing symptoms like bloating, cramping, loose stools, or a sudden need to use the bathroom. and seniors who already have a reduced ability to clear crystal forming compounds are at significantly higher risk of oxalate buildup. This doesn't mean spinach is bad nor that beetroot should be avoided. It simply means they should not be juiced together, especially in large quantities. If you enjoy leafy greens with your beet juice, choose low oxilate options such as kale, mint, celery leaves, or romaine. If you love spinach, have it cooked or eaten separately at a different time of day, not blended raw with beets. Your kidneys work hard every minute of your life. This is one combination they shouldn't have to battle against. Combination number seven, mixing beet juice with carrots. Beets and carrots look like the perfect duo. two bright nutrient-rich roots often paired in juices, smoothies, and detox blends. On their own, each vegetable is incredibly healthy. But when juiced together in concentrated form, especially raw, they create a quiet overload your kidneys, liver, and blood sugar simply cannot handle. Both beets and carrots are high oxilate root vegetables. When consumed separately in moderate portions, oxalates are manageable for most people. But when you combine them into one powerful juice, the oxalate concentration skyrockets. This dramatically increases the risk of calcium oxilate kidney stones, especially in seniors, diabetics, dehydrated individuals, and anyone with even mild kidney impairment. The oxalates from both roots bind tightly to calcium, forming sharp crystals that accumulate silently until they trigger pain, inflammation, or urinary complications. The second issue is blood sugar load. Beets and carrots both contain naturally occurring sugars. When you juice them, you remove the fiber that slows absorption, meaning those sugars hit your bloodstream rapidly. For people with insulin resistance, pre-diabetes, fatty liver, or sluggish metabolism, this combination can cause noticeable glucose spikes, followed by fatigue, cravings, and increased inflammatory stress. Over time, these swings make blood sugar harder to control and can worsen metabolic conditions. Some people also notice digestive discomfort after drinking this mix. Without fiber to buffer the sugars and oxalates, the juice fermentss quickly in the gut, creating gas, bloating, and stomach heaviness. Seniors with slower digestion or low stomach acid are especially prone to these symptoms. Again, the mistake isn't the vegetables, it's the concentrated combination. Each root is healthy when eaten whole, cooked, or enjoyed separately on alternate days. But routinely juicing them together compresses too much sugar, too many oxalates, and too little fiber into a single drink your body wasn't designed to process. A safer approach is simple. If you're drinking beet juice today, skip the carrot. And if you want carrot juice, enjoy it alone or blended with low oxilate, low sugar addins like cucumber, celery, or a small piece of green apple. This way you support your kidneys, protect your blood sugar, and still enjoy the nutritional power of both roots, just not in the same glass. How to drink beet juice the right way. Now that you know which combinations silently cancel out beets incredible healing power, let's shift to what actually works. Beet juice is one of the most potent natural remedies for circulation, energy, liver support, and blood pressure. but only when you drink it correctly. Most people make two mistakes. They drink too much and they mix it with the wrong ingredients. Your body doesn't need a giant detox blast. It needs the right dose at the right time prepared the right way. Number one, respect the dose. One medium beat per day is enough. Beets are rich in natural nitrates which turn into nitric oxide, improving blood flow and lowering blood pressure. But too much nitrate or too many oxilates overload the kidneys and may cause dizziness or stomach discomfort. One medium bead or about 100 to 120 ml of fresh juice is the ideal amount for daily use. Enough to boost energy and circulation without stressing your system. Number two, drink it pure. No sugar, no sweeteners. Beet juice works best when it's clean. Adding refined sugar, honey, or healthy sweeteners spikes blood glucose and weakens the effect of nitric oxide. Your body absorbs nutrients more efficiently when the flavor is natural. If the earthy taste feels strong, blend with cucumber, celery, or a small piece of green apple. gentle options that support digestion without wiping out the benefits. Number three, drink it on an empty, light stomach. The best time is first thing in the morning or 30 minutes before lunch. When your stomach is light, nitric oxide conversion is stronger, digestion is smoother, and the energizing effects are more noticeable. Avoid drinking beet juice right after heavy meals. Slow digestion blocks how quickly the nutrients can activate. Number four, fresh is best. Don't store it for long. Beet nutrients begin oxidizing within minutes. Stored juice loses potency and may start fermenting, especially after 12 to 24 hours. Freshly extracted beet juice delivers the strongest nitric oxide boost, the best liver supporting betaene, and the fullest antioxidant punch. For maximum benefit, drink it within 10 to 15 minutes of making it. Number five, sip slowly, don't gulp. Beet juice isn't a shot. It's a functional tonic. Sipping slowly allows your digestive system to process nitrates steadily without overwhelming your stomach or bloodstream. Seniors especially notice smoother blood pressure response when drinking beet juice gradually. Number six, rotate it. Don't drink the same blend every day. Your kidneys, liver, and gut benefit from variety. Rotate beet juice with low oxilate options like celery, cucumber, or leafy greens. This protects your mineral balance and reduces the long-term buildup of oxalates. When consumed in the right way, pure, moderate, fresh, and at the right time, beet juice becomes a powerful daily ritual that supports your heart, brain, energy, and detox systems without the risks of overload or nutrient interference. Conclusion: Beet juice can be one of the most transformative health habits you add to your day, boosting circulation, supporting your liver, increasing energy, and helping your heart work more efficiently. But as you've seen today, how you drink it matters just as much as drinking it at all. The wrong combinations can trigger fermentation, irritate your stomach, block nutrient absorption, and even raise your risk of kidney stones. The right method turns a simple root into a powerful natural therapy. So the next time you prepare beet juice, remember, keep it pure, keep it fresh, keep it simple. One medium beat, the right timing, and no harmful mixins. Let this juice nourish you the way nature intended. Not just to support the channel, but to let YouTube know that real senior focused health education matters. Share this video with someone who drinks beet juice daily. Choose wisely, choose simply, and choose vitality.

segments.text: Did you know that one of the healthiest
juices on the planet can become
dangerous with just a single wrong
ingredient? Many people start their
morning feeling proud. They juice a
fresh beet, pour it into a glass, and
believe they're fueling their heart,
cleansing their liver, and boosting
their energy for the day. But what
almost no one realizes is that beets are
powerful. And like anything powerful,
they require respect. Inside this humble
red root are compounds that can
transform your circulation, open your
blood vessels, strengthen your liver,
and supercharge stamina. Yet, those very
compounds also react strongly when mixed
with the wrong foods. And that's where
silent trouble begins.
Every day, thousands of people
unknowingly create beet juice
combinations that irritate the stomach,
cause fermentation, raise oxalate
levels, overload the liver, interfere
with circulation, and even worse, cancel
out the very nutrients they're trying to
absorb. The scariest part, most of these
harmful mixtures look healthy on the
surface. Many are popular recipes
printed in cookbooks, shared on social
media, or recommended by friends who
simply don't understand what's happening
inside the body. But today, you will. In
this video, we're going to uncover seven
beet juice combinations you must never
make, including one that can be
genuinely dangerous for anyone over 50
or living with kidney or digestive
issues. And more importantly, I'll show
you exactly how to drink beet juice the
right way so your heart, liver,
circulation, and energy all benefit
without risk. Stay with me to the end.
What you learn today might completely
change the way you prepare this healing
juice forever.
Combination number one, mixing beet
juice with lemon. One of the most common
mistakes people make is adding a big
squeeze of lemon to their beet juice. It
sounds healthy, right? Vitamin C,
freshness, detox. What could possibly be
wrong? Quite a lot, actually. Lemon is
extremely acidic. And when that strong
acidity collides with the natural
compounds inside raw beetroot, the pH of
the juice changes instantly. For many
seniors, especially those with
gastritis, acid reflux, or a sensitive
stomach, this can trigger irritation
within minutes. People often report
heartburn, nausea, or a sour stomach
shortly after drinking this combo
without realizing the culprit is the
lemon. But the bigger problem is what
happens to beetroot's nutrients. Beets
are naturally rich in plant-based iron
and beneficial pigments that support
blood building and circulation.
Those nutrients require a stable
internal environment to be absorbed
properly. When lemon overly acidifies
the mixture, it disrupts that process.
Instead of enhancing absorption, the
acidity actually interferes with it,
especially iron absorption, cancelling
one of the key benefits people expect
from beet juice. What was supposed to
support energy and oxygenation can end
up giving you nothing but discomfort.
There's also the issue of dizziness or
weakness some people feel afterward.
That's because the acidic shock can
overstimulate stomach acid production,
especially in older adults whose
digestive lining is already thinner or
more fragile. And if you're dealing with
reflux, this combination can aggravate
the esophagus further. Lemon itself
isn't the villain. It's the quantity. A
small splash for flavor can be harmless.
But large squeezes, half a lemon, or the
juice of a whole lemon, is where
problems begin. If you enjoy brightness
in your beet juice, use moderation. Just
a few drops for freshness, never a heavy
squeeze. And if your stomach is
sensitive, skip the lemon entirely. Your
body will thank you.
Combination number two, mixing beet
juice with honey or sugar. This is one
of the most common mistakes people make
and unfortunately it is also one of the
most harmful. Many people don't enjoy
the earthy taste of beetroot. So they
try to fix it by adding honey, table
sugar or even sweet syrups. It feels
innocent. After all, honey is natural,
right? But the moment you add
concentrated sugars to beet juice, you
completely change how the body processes
it. Beets are naturally rich in nitrates
that convert into nitric oxide, a
compound that relaxes blood vessels,
supports circulation, boosts stamina,
and helps regulate blood pressure. But
when you combine beet juice with honey
or sugar, that nitrate conversion is
disrupted. The result, the very benefit
most people drink beet juice for
improved blood flow becomes
significantly weaker. What you're left
with is a sugary drink that spikes blood
glucose and offers only a fraction of
the cardiovascular support beets are
meant to deliver. For seniors, this
sugar nitrate conflict is even more
serious. Added sugars cause a rapid
insulin response followed by
inflammation and oxidative stress,
especially in those with diabetes,
pre-diabetes, liver issues, or metabolic
syndrome. Honey, despite being natural,
still behaves like a quick sugar in the
bloodstream. This can accelerate fat
buildup in the liver, worsen insulin
resistance, and trigger energy crashes.
And for those with high creatinine or
kidney concerns, sugar overload forces
the kidneys to work harder, the opposite
of what your body needs. The good news,
beetroot already has a mild natural
sweetness when prepared correctly. Most
people simply need time for their taste
buds to adjust. But if you truly need to
soften the flavor, choose low impact
companions like green apple or cucumber.
They add freshness, fiber, and hydration
without interfering with nitrates or
stressing your blood sugar. In short,
sugar and beet juice don't belong
together. If you want beet juice to act
like a natural remedy, keep it pure.
Your heart, liver, and energy levels
will notice the difference.
Combination number three, mixing beet
juice with orange. On the surface, this
combination sounds healthy. Two vitamin
rich fruits blended into one refreshing
drink. But for many people, especially
seniors or anyone with digestive
sensitivity, mixing beet juice with
orange can quickly backfire. The issue
begins with acidity. Oranges are highly
acidic and when their acids meet the
natural sugars and compounds in
beetroot, the result is a recipe for
intestinal fermentation. This means the
mixture begins producing excess gas in
the gut leading to bloating, cramping,
and uncomfortable abdominal pressure.
For people with gastritis, acid reflux,
or IBS, this reaction can feel almost
immediate. But digestion isn't the only
concern. Oranges contain large amounts
of vitamin C. And while vitamin C is
essential, too much of it in a single
drink can interfere with nitrate
absorption. The very compounds in
beetroot responsible for improving
circulation and lowering blood pressure.
When vitamin C dominates the blend, the
nitrates convert less efficiently into
nitric oxide, which reduces the
cardiovascular benefits of beet juice
dramatically. You think you're
supporting your heart, but the
combination quietly cancels out the
effect. This mix also places extra
demand on older digestive systems. The
natural sugars in both beets and oranges
digest quickly when juiced and without
their original fiber structure. They
rush into the bloodstream. This can
spike blood glucose more suddenly than
people expect, especially in seniors,
those with pre-diabetes, or individuals
with fluctuating blood pressure. If you
enjoy citrus, it's far safer to consume
beet juice and orange juice at separate
times of the day, giving your stomach
and circulatory system space to process
each one properly. Beet juice works best
on a relatively empty stomach, while
orange juice is best taken earlier in
the day with food to buffer its acidity.
So, while the bright color and taste may
look appealing, beet plus orange is one
combination that does more harm than
good, especially when consistency,
digestion, and heart health matter most.
Combination number four, mixing beet
juice with vinegar. This may seem like a
harmless blend. After all, vinegar shows
up in detox drinks, homemade tonics, and
countless health hacks. But when it
comes to beet juice, pairing it with
vinegar is one of the biggest mistakes
you can make. Beetroot is naturally rich
in nitrates, compounds that convert into
nitric oxide and help open your blood
vessels, improve circulation, and
support healthy blood pressure. Vinegar,
however, is extremely acidic, and that
acidity can destroy a significant
portion of these nitrates before your
body even has a chance to use them. In
other words, you lose the primary
benefit that makes beet juice so
valuable. But the problems go deeper
than nutrient loss. The high acetic acid
content in vinegar can irritate the
lining of the stomach when combined with
raw beet juice, especially for people
who already deal with reflux, gastritis,
or a sensitive digestive system. Many
seniors describe a burning sensation,
nausea, or a wave of stomach heaviness
shortly after drinking this mix. And
because both beetroot and vinegar
influence blood pressure, beat by
lowering it, vinegar by altering sodium
and potassium balance, the combination
can cause unexpected dips, dizziness, or
weakness in people with naturally low
blood pressure. For anyone trying to
detox, adding vinegar may feel like the
right move, but the truth is the
opposite. Instead of helping the liver
work more efficiently, the acidity makes
the liver work harder to rebalance your
internal pH. That means more metabolic
stress, not less. And for people with
kidney issues, this extra acid load
becomes even more problematic, forcing
the kidneys to neutralize compounds that
the body should never have had to deal
with in the first place. The best
approach is simple. Keep beet juice
pure. If you want a lighter, more
refreshing texture, use water, cucumber,
or a low acid fruit like green apple.
Vinegar has its own benefits, just not
when mixed with beetroot. Use it in
salad dressings or cooking, but keep it
far away from your morning beet juice if
you want to protect your stomach, your
blood pressure, and the very nutrients
that make beetroot such a healing food.
Combination number five, mixing beet
juice with milk or dairy products. Some
people try blending beet juice with milk
to make it creamier, but this is one of
the worst combinations you can make. On
paper, it may seem harmless. Beets for
nutrients, milk for protein. Yet inside
your body, this mix triggers reactions
that lead to digestive distress,
nutrient loss, and inflammation. The
issue begins with protein acid
interaction. Beetroot is naturally
acidic, and when beet juice comes into
contact with milk's proteins, especially
casein, it causes them to curdle
prematurely. This curdling doesn't
happen gently like yogurt fermentation.
It happens suddenly inside your stomach.
The result, heavy digestion,
fermentation, and uncomfortable symptoms
like bloating, gas, reflux, and a rock
in the stomach feeling that can last for
hours. But there's more. Milk requires a
very specific digestive environment,
while beetroot stimulates stomach acids
and bile flow. When these two processes
clash, your body struggles to break down
both foods properly. That means you
absorb less calcium from the milk and
fewer vitamins from the beetroot. The
exact opposite of what most people
intend when they make this healthy
smoothie. For people with lactose
intolerance or slow digestion, which
becomes more common after age 50, this
combination often leads to nausea,
cramping, or rapid onset of acidity.
This pairing can also overload the
liver. Beet juice increases nitric oxide
activity and improves circulation, while
dairy slows digestion and thickens
stomach contents. When consumed
together, the liver must work harder to
filter metabolic byproducts from both
foods at once, increasing inflammation
instead of reducing it. For people with
fatty liver, sluggish digestion, or a
history of reflux, this combination
should be avoided altogether. If you
want a creamier beet drink, there are
safer alternatives. Coconut water, for
example, hydrates, supports
electrolytes, and blends beautifully
with beetroot without causing digestive
conflict. Cucumber also adds smoothness
and freshness while supporting kidney
and liver cleansing. These pairings help
your body work more efficiently, not
harder. Dairy has its place, just not
inside your beet juice. Keep them
separate, and your stomach, liver, and
energy levels will thank you.
Combination number six, mixing beet
juice with spinach.
On their own, beets and spinach are
nutritional powerhouses. But when you
put them together in the same juice,
especially in concentrated raw form, you
accidentally create a biochemical
problem your kidneys and bones don't
appreciate. Both beets and spinach are
extremely high in oxalates, natural
plant compounds that bind to minerals
like calcium, magnesium, and iron. When
consumed in large amounts or paired
together, oxalates can overwhelm your
body's ability to process and eliminate
them. The biggest risk, kidney stones.
Oxalates from both foods join with
calcium in the kidneys and form calcium
oxilate crystals. The most common and
most painful type of kidney stone for
people with a history of stones, reduced
kidney function, mild dehydration, or
even low magnesium intake. This
combination can quickly push the body
into a high oxalate overload. You don't
feel it immediately, but the crystals
silently accumulate, increasing your
risk week after week. Another hidden
problem is mineral blocking. Oxalates
grab onto important nutrients,
especially calcium and iron, making them
harder for your body to absorb. So,
while many people combine beets and
spinach thinking they're supercharging
their juice, the reality is the
opposite. You absorb less nutrition, and
your digestive system has to work harder
to eliminate what it can't use. Over
time, this can weaken bones, worsen
anemia, or contribute to fatigue and
muscle weakness. People with gut issues
such as IBS, gastritis, or low stomach
acid are especially vulnerable. High
oxilate juices pass through the
digestive system too quickly, irritating
the intestine and causing symptoms like
bloating, cramping, loose stools, or a
sudden need to use the bathroom. and
seniors who already have a reduced
ability to clear crystal forming
compounds are at significantly higher
risk of oxalate buildup.
This doesn't mean spinach is bad nor
that beetroot should be avoided. It
simply means they should not be juiced
together, especially in large
quantities. If you enjoy leafy greens
with your beet juice, choose low oxilate
options such as kale, mint, celery
leaves, or romaine. If you love spinach,
have it cooked or eaten separately at a
different time of day, not blended raw
with beets. Your kidneys work hard every
minute of your life. This is one
combination they shouldn't have to
battle against.
Combination number seven, mixing beet
juice with carrots. Beets and carrots
look like the perfect duo. two bright
nutrient-rich roots often paired in
juices, smoothies, and detox blends. On
their own, each vegetable is incredibly
healthy. But when juiced together in
concentrated form, especially raw, they
create a quiet overload your kidneys,
liver, and blood sugar simply cannot
handle. Both beets and carrots are high
oxilate root vegetables. When consumed
separately in moderate portions,
oxalates are manageable for most people.
But when you combine them into one
powerful juice, the oxalate
concentration skyrockets. This
dramatically increases the risk of
calcium oxilate kidney stones,
especially in seniors, diabetics,
dehydrated individuals, and anyone with
even mild kidney impairment. The
oxalates from both roots bind tightly to
calcium, forming sharp crystals that
accumulate silently until they trigger
pain, inflammation, or urinary
complications.
The second issue is blood sugar load.
Beets and carrots both contain naturally
occurring sugars. When you juice them,
you remove the fiber that slows
absorption, meaning those sugars hit
your bloodstream rapidly. For people
with insulin resistance, pre-diabetes,
fatty liver, or sluggish metabolism,
this combination can cause noticeable
glucose spikes, followed by fatigue,
cravings, and increased inflammatory
stress. Over time, these swings make
blood sugar harder to control and can
worsen metabolic conditions. Some people
also notice digestive discomfort after
drinking this mix. Without fiber to
buffer the sugars and oxalates, the
juice fermentss quickly in the gut,
creating gas, bloating, and stomach
heaviness. Seniors with slower digestion
or low stomach acid are especially prone
to these symptoms. Again, the mistake
isn't the vegetables, it's the
concentrated combination. Each root is
healthy when eaten whole, cooked, or
enjoyed separately on alternate days.
But routinely juicing them together
compresses too much sugar, too many
oxalates, and too little fiber into a
single drink your body wasn't designed
to process. A safer approach is simple.
If you're drinking beet juice today,
skip the carrot. And if you want carrot
juice, enjoy it alone or blended with
low oxilate, low sugar addins like
cucumber, celery, or a small piece of
green apple. This way you support your
kidneys, protect your blood sugar, and
still enjoy the nutritional power of
both roots, just not in the same glass.
How to drink beet juice the right way.
Now that you know which combinations
silently cancel out beets incredible

When consumed in the right way, pure,

videos.title: Should you drink BEETROOT JUICE?

transcript.fullText: Last year, I uploaded a short video explaining why beetroot juice might not be for everybody. And honestly, that video received a lot of backlash and trolling. So, today's video is my attempt to give you a complete and sciencebacked explanation of what the real issue is with raw vegetable juices, especially made with beetroot and spinach. I genuinely urge you to watch this video till the end because this topic is not black and white. I'm going to answer if vegetable juices are really worth it. We're going to talk about risks, benefits, who should completely avoid vegetable juices, and how can you enjoy the benefits of vegetables while avoiding the risks. I'm going to base today's video on two kinds of evidence. Number one, experimental evidence. What we know from scientific studies, case studies, and lab findings. Number two, clinical evidence. What doctors, dieticians, and practitioners observe repeatedly in real people over time. Before we start, I am Nupama. I'm a PhD in food science and a certified nutrition coach. If you're new here, make sure to subscribe to my channel. And if you want to book a personal consultation with me, the link is in the description box. The main compounds of interest for us in vegetables are vitamin C, B vitamins, antioxidants like polyphenols and betaarotene, fiber, and oxalates. Let's see what science has to say about these in juices and cooked vegetables. Let's talk about vitamin C and polyphenols. As compared to their cooked versions, raw juices contain more vitamin C and certain antioxidants like polyphenols. Because vitamin C is heat sensitive, cooking reduces its level. Therefore, the higher vitamin C and antioxidants in raw juices is definitely an advantage. But here's the thing most people don't understand. Nutrient content is not the same as nutrient absorption. Which brings me to something called bioavailability. How much does your body actually absorb? Even if a juice contains large amounts of vitamin C and antioxidants, your body might not absorb all of it because absorption depends on the dose of the nutrient, your gut health and inflammation in your digestive tract. Our intestine has transport systems that help us absorb these nutrients and these transport systems tend to get saturated which means that even if you keep increasing the dosage, you might not absorb all of it. Sometimes the excess just gets excreted through the urine. So the more vitamin C in vegetable juices does not necessarily mean an advantage or mean better. Now here's something interesting and very important. Certain vitamins like vitamin B1 and some antioxidants like betaarotene which is a precursor to vitamin A are better absorbed and are more bioavailable to our body when the vegetables are cooked. So vegetables like carrots, spinach and tomatoes can actually provide better absorption of certain nutrients when they are consumed in their cooked form because cooking helps break down the plant cell walls. So the argument is not raw is good and cooked is bad. The reality is that it depends on the nutrient. Raw vegetable juices give us better vitamin C and antioxidants while cooked vegetables give us better access to nutrients like vitamin B1 and beta. Coming to fiber, a major issue with the beetroot or spinach juice is that during juicing the fiber is removed and fiber is one of the main reasons that vegetables are beneficial. Fiber helps reduce cholesterol absorption, slow down blood sugar spikes in the body, improve our gut health, and feed the healthy gut bacteria. So, if you're drinking only the juice, you are getting some micronutrients, but you're missing out on the metabolic benefits that we get from consuming the whole vegetable. Now I'm going to talk about the most controversial compound in beetroot and spinach juice. Oxalates. Oxalates are natural compounds present in many plants. Plants use them as a protective mechanism to protect themselves from the attack of pests or predators. Oxalates are present in heavy amounts in beetroot, spinach or even ashcord juice. The problem in humans is that oxalates act as anti-nutrients, meaning they bind to certain minerals like calcium or iron and lower their absorption in the body. This might be of concern in populations like women who might be anemic or have a higher iron requirement in people who need more calcium in the diet or in people with poor mineral status. Now here comes into picture clinical evidence or real world observations by doctors or dieticians who suggest that not everybody might be sensitive to oxalate in the same way. Your response to oxalates might depend on your genetics, your gut microbiome, digestive inflammation and the health of your liver and kidneys. Some people might absorb more oxalates than others and for them drinking excessive vegetable juices of spinach, beetroot or ashcot puts them at a risk of kidney stone formation, bloating, gut irritation and inflammation. There's also an observation that people who have IBS- like symptoms, bloating, joint pains or other inflammatory conditions might feel better on a low oxalate diet. And cooking, boiling, steaming significantly reduces the oxalate levels of vegetables. So what does all this mean for you? Are vegetable juices worth it? Raw vegetable juices might provide a quick boost of vitamin C and some antioxidants. But there are also tradeoffs like the fiber is removed. Absorption of nutrients has its limits and oxalate load becomes concentrated in raw vegetable juices. Cooking reduces vitamin C. Yes, but it increases the bioavailability of other nutrients like beta carotene. Fiber is not lost during cooking. And most importantly, cooking reduces oxalates. For most healthy people, an occasional beetroot or spinach juice is absolutely fine. But are vegetable juices worth the hype? They are not. They are not the miracle or superfood that social media makes them out to be. In fact, if you're someone who has a history of kidney stones, or if you suffer from digestive issues or bloating, liver or kidney dysfunction, or if you have low iron levels, drinking raw vegetable juices is absolutely not recommended for you. A smarter thing for you to do is to cook your vegetables. Keep the fiber in your diet which comes from whole foods. And if you're worried about the loss of vitamin C during cooking, you can add it separately in your diet through the use of lemons, amla, fruits like berries, citrus fruits or guavas. Steaming is a great middle option. It is much better than boiling vegetables. Steaming helps preserve a lot of the nutrients while lowering oxalates. I hope this video was useful. If it was, do let me know. And if you disagree to my opinion, I am open to discussion. But just make sure that you watch the entire video before writing down a comment. If you want more evidence-based nutrition content without fear-mongering, do not forget to subscribe to my channel and share this video with somebody who might need it. I will see you guys in my next video. Take care until then. Bye.

transcript.fullText: [Music] now that it's the new year everyone's talking about detoxing and cleansing my personal belief is that a healthy diet is built from one day to the next from one meal to another without guilt or having to go on an extreme diet but i also know there are times when we can over indulge in foods like sugar or alcohol and so going on a short detoxifying diet or even incorporating more detoxifying foods can help feel us more energetic and healthy overall so i am really excited to share this beet juice recipe using a juicer today a few videos ago i did share my beet juice recipe using a blender and i got a few comments that said that it was really messy and that i should be using a juicer and i actually do have a juicer but i did want to show in that video how you can make juice even if you don't have a juicer so today i'll be using my omega masticating juicer and having a juicer is definitely the easiest and most efficient way to make homemade vegetable juice and if you're new to juicing or you're just still just thinking about getting a juicer i want to point you to an article on my website cleaneatingkitchen.com that goes through i think four different types of juicers and how to pick the right one for your needs and your budget and i will link that article in the description box below so let's get into making this beet juice recipe so the first step to making any type of homemade vegetable juice is having to prep your vegetables and since we're making beet juice today of course i'm starting with three large organic red beets and the first step is to prep the beets by pulling off the beet greens i just twist them off using my hands and you can save your beet greens i personally don't care for beet greens i find them to be too bitter but i know a lot of people use them in stir fries or you could even steam them and use them in a green smoothie next we're going to put the beets in a colander and then just use a sponge to scrub any dirt off of the beets the next step in this vegetable juice recipe is to wash a bunch of carrots and if your carrots come with greens you can also just pull those greens off and again you can use them in recipes i usually don't use them they're usually really full of dirt but you can they are edible the next step is to wash our apples and today i'm using i think i'm using fuji apples or honey crisp those are my two favorite apple types you could also use green apples and you can use either one apple or two apples or leave the apples out to all together the apples are the richest source of natural sugars in this juice recipe so if you're on a very low sugar diet then you can just leave the apples out all together i'm using two apples because the sweeter i make the juice the more likely it is that i can get my husband to drink it then we're going to wash the lemon or the lime you can use either one in this recipe then we're going to prep our celery for juicing by cutting off each end of the celery and then and then washing the celery ribs and then we're just going to give a big bunch of flat leaf parsley a good rinse after we all have all our fruits and vegetables washed then it's time to prep them and get them all into pieces that will fit through the feeding tube of our juicer so first i cut my apples into thin slices my feeding tube for my omega juicer is really only about an inch and a half wide so i do have to make sure to cut my veggies into pieces that will fit into that feeding tube and then next we're gonna cut our carrots into pieces and i just like to cut the tops and the bottoms off you don't need to peel the carrots generally i almost always use organic carrots so i don't generally peel them but just cut off the tops and the bottoms and then use the center part and then to prep the parsley for juicing i just cut off the stems and i use like the top two thirds of the parsley and then as far as using ginger in this recipe it's one of my favorite ingredients i highly suggest including it and i just like to cut off the skin from the ginger and then i end up with about a one inch piece in total for this recipe and then to prep your lemon or lime which adds really nice brightness to the recipe just cut the skin off and then we're just gonna cut the actual fruit of the lemon or lime into pieces for the juice and then of course the last ingredient are beets which are the star of this juice recipe i just cut them into thin slices that will fit into the juicer so let's move over to my table where i have my juicer set up and we will get on to making this juice okay i've got everything set up so i'm going to turn on the juicer i'm going to alternate feeding the hard veggies with the soft veggies into my juicer and then um we'll just have to run everything through and then i'll meet you at the end and we'll give the juice a [Music] taste [Applause] we got a lot of pulp from this recipe i ended up getting two containers and as i mentioned in one of my previous juicing videos you can save this pulp you can use it for other recipes you can just search online for juice pulp recipes and then we got a ton of juice this juice is going to be so delicious and just be warned no matter what kind of juice you use if you use beets it's going to turn in a beautiful color of red and this one is just beautiful i love the color and i can't wait to taste it and share it with you once you finish juicing all the fruits and vegetables i like to pour the juice into a jar which i store in the refrigerator for up to two or three days and then if the juice separates i just give it a shake before i serve it so let's give this homemade beet juice recipe a taste we made it using my masticating juicer and i think it's going to be delicious let me give it a taste we get that zesty flavor of the ginger at the end and it is sweet because of the carrots and the apples i really can't taste the celery or the parsley and i can't even really taste the beets that much it is just like a sweet and spicy juice i know it's gonna be so healthy and detoxifying and cleansing for the new year so i hope that you love this recipe and please check out my playlist of some of my other juicing recipes and i'll see you in the next video bye [Music] everybody

videos.title: What Are The Side Effects Of Beetroot Juice? - Cardiology Community

transcript.fullText: What are the side effects of beetroot juice? Have you ever wondered about the side effects of beetroot juice, especially when it comes to heart health? Beetroot juice is often praised for its potential benefits in cardiovascular wellness. It is rich in nitrates which can convert to nitric oxide in the body. This process helps to widen blood vessels and improve blood flow making it a popular choice for managing blood pressure. However, it is important to be aware of the possible side effects that can come with its consumption. One significant side effect is blood pressure changes. While beetroot juice can help lower high blood pressure, it may cause a dangerous drop in blood pressure for those who already have low blood pressure or are on blood pressure medications. This can lead to dizziness, lightadedness, or even fainting. If you are taking medication for blood pressure, it is wise to consult a health care provider before adding beetroot juice to your diet. Another common issue is digestive discomfort. Beetroot juice is quite acidic and consuming it in large amounts or on an empty stomach can result in bloating, gas, and abdominal pain. If you are new to beetroot juice, it might be best to start with small servings to see how your body reacts. Kidney stone risk is another consideration. Beetroot contains a high level of oxalates which can bind with calcium in the body and from calcium oxalate crystals. This can increase the risk of kidney stones for individuals who are prone to them. If you have a history of kidney stones, it is advisable to limit your intake of beetroot juice. Additionally, there are concerns regarding hair loss. The oxalates in beetroot can interfere with the absorption of essential minerals like zinc which is vital for hair growth. A high intake of oxalates may lead to zinc deficiency potentially resulting in hair loss. Lastly, there are some concerns about the formation of nitroso compounds which are known to be carcinogenic. However, more research is needed to fully understand this risk. In summary, while beetroot juice can be beneficial for heart disease prevention and blood pressure management, it is essential to be aware of these potential side effects. Always consult with a health care provider, especially if you have existing health conditions or are on medication. This way, you can enjoy the benefits of beetroot juice while minimizing any risks.

variety or it could have even been detox symptoms from foods that I've ate before trying the beat so who knows I got much more experience under my belt now to determine whether a fruit or vegetable is suitable for me I like to try it alone on an empty stomach this method gives me a clear understanding of its effects based on my body's response trying it by itself is one of the best ways not only to transcript.fullText: अगर आप हेल्थ के नाम पर बीटर रूट यानी चुकंदर को सैलेड या जूस की फॉर्म में कंज्यूम करते हो तो आज ही करना बंद कर दो। रिसेंटली आप में से काफी सारे लोगों ने मुझे यह वीडियो भेजा। चुकंदर तो किसी को बिल्कुल भी नहीं खानी चाहिए। बीट रूट बहुत ही खतरनाक चीज होती है। बिल्कुल भी ना खाएं। सर ने मैसेज तो सही दिया बट रीजन एक्सप्लेन नहीं किया। देखो सबसे पहले तो चुकंदर एक रूट वेजिटेबल है जो डायरेक्टली सोइल के अंदर ग्रो होता है। इसलिए रॉ बीटर रूट में डेंजरस बैक्टीरिया, वायरस एंड पैरासाइट्स प्रेजेंट होते हैं। जो डायरिया, वोमिटिंग, फूड पोइजनिंग और मिसकैरज तक कॉज कर सकते हैं। सेकंड हार्मफुल माइक्रोब्स के अलावा रॉ बीटर रूट में हैवी मेटल्स एंड पेस्टिसाइड्स प्रेजेंट होते हैं। जो हमारे वाइटल ऑर्गन्स जैसे लिवर, किडनीज एंड हार्ट को डैमेज कर सकते हैं। थर्ड रॉ बीटर रूट में ऑक्सिलेट्स हाई अमाउंट में प्रेजेंट होते हैं। ऑक्सिलेट एक तरह के एंटीट्रिएंट्स होते हैं जो फूड में प्रेजेंट कैल्शियम के साथ बाइंड करते हैं। जिससे बॉडी में कैल्शियम तो कम अब्सॉर्ब होता ही है। साथ ही इससे किडनी में कैल्शियम ऑक्सिलेट स्टोंस होने का भी रिस्क इंक्रीस होता है। फोर्थ रॉ बीटर रूट हाई फोड मैप होते हैं। इसलिए आईबीएस और आईबीडी के पेशेंट्स को तो बिल्कुल भी रॉ बीटर रूट नहीं खाने चाहिए। बाकी लोगों में भी यह डाइजेस्टिव इश्यूज जैसे ब्लोटिंग और गैस कॉज कर सकते हैं। और बहुत लोगों को लगता है कि चुकंदर खाने से उन्हें आयरन मिलता है। लेकिन 100 ग्राम बीटर रूट में एक माइक्रो ग्राम से भी कम आयरन होता है। इससे कई ज्यादा आयरन तो आपको स्पिनच एंड पंपकिन सीड्स में मिल जाएगा। तो क्या बीटर रूट खाना ही छोड़ दें? बिल्कुल नहीं। आप बीटर रूट को प्रॉपर्ली वॉश, पील, बॉईल और कुक करके कंज्यूम करो। ऐसा करने से रॉ बीटर रूट के हार्मफुल कंपाउंड्स तो डिस्ट्रॉय हो जाएंगे। साथ ही आपको डाइजेस्टिव इश्यूज नहीं होंगे और बीटर रूट में प्रेजेंट बेनिफिशियल न्यूट्रिएंट्स भी आपकी बॉडी में इजीली अब्सॉर्ब हो जाएंगे।

Stay with me until the end because that rule alone could explain why everything you've tried before hasn't delivered the results you were hoping for. After 50, your body's natural nitric oxide production drops sharply. And it starts with a humble dark red root that's been sitting in cardiovascular research labs for decades and the three ingredients that make it exponentially more powerful. All within just 2 to 3 hours of consumption with effects that last up to a full 24 hours. Beet juice works. The science is undeniable. But here's what I need you to understand. उसकी जरूरत नहीं है। अगर आपको लगता है कि टेस्ट अच्छा नहीं आएगा तो थोड़ी बहुत आप चीनी इसमें ऐड कर सकते हैं। उसके बाद हम इसको स्टेन कर लेंगे। ये जो इसका फाइबर वगैरह है, इसको हम साइड पे कर लेंगे। और अब हमने क्या करना है? इसके अंदर कुछ चीजें ऐड करेंगे। तो पहले तो हम इसमें बर्फ ऐड कर देते हैं क्योंकि गर्मियां है तो इसको ठंडा-ठंडा हम सर्व करेंगे। इसमें हम काला नमक ऐड करेंगे हाफ टीस्पून के करीब। इससे ज्यादा आपने ऐड नहीं करना। लेमन हमें अच्छी क्वालिटी के यहां पे चाहिए जूस से भरे हुए। दो लेमन हमने इसके अंदर जो है कट करके ऐड कर देने हैं। याद रखिएगा लेमन आप जब भी खरीदें तो एक लेमन इस तरह काट के चेक कर लिया करें। आजकल मार्केट में जो लेमन आ रहे हैं उनके अंदर जूस नहीं है। ये क्वालिटी आपने परचेस करनी है। जहां से मिले महंगे मिले डजंट मैटर। आपने यही क्वालिटी लेनी है। इन्हीं के अंदर जूस होता है। अब हम क्या करेंगे? दो से तीन लेमन का जूस इसके अंदर ऐड कर देंगे। इस ड्रिंक को आप किसी भी टाइम पी सकते हैं। किसी भी ऐज का बंदा बंदी पी सकते हैं। कोई लिमिटेशन नहीं है। दिन में कम से कम एक गिलास पिए। और अगर आपने यह एक महीना पी लिया तो यकीन करें आप मुझे दुआएं देंगे। आपका रंग, आपकी सेहत, आपकी तंदुरुस्ती इस लेवल पे चली जाएगी कि क्या ही बात है। और हां, यह बहुत ठंडी तासीर वाला है। तो आप इसको गर्मियों में पिए। आपको सर् गर्मी भी नहीं लगेगी और आपकी इसी का बिल भी जो है वो कम हो जाएगा। तो इसको आपने इस तरह से ठंडा चिल सर्व करना है। टेस्ट में यह थोड़ा सा आपको शायद इतना पसंद ना आए तो इसके लिए आप चीनी ऐड कर सकते हैं लेकिन आपको करने की जरूरत नहीं है। ऐसे ही एक ग्लास भर के आप पिए पूरी फैमिली को पिलाएं इंशाल्लाह विदिन वन मंथ आप जो है वो दुआएं देंगे कि इतनी शानदार ड्रिंक आपको पहले क्यों नहीं पता थी। फिर से बता दूं यह जिगर के लिए, स्टमक के लिए, खून के लिए, आपकी किडनीज़ के लिए, आपकी जो एक हेल्थ होती है उसके लिए ओवरऑल बहुत ही बेनिफिशियल है। दुआ में रखिए। वीडियो को लाइक कीजिए। ज्यादा से ज्यादा दीजिए तक। अल्लाह हाफिज।
