videos.title: 1 Cup Beet Juice Before Bedtime DAILY Will Trigger These 8 Things To Your Body!

transcript.fullText:
Drink 1 Cup Beet Juice Before Bed For 
30 Days, See What Happens To Your Body! If you’re like me, always looking 
for natural ways to sleep better, feel more energized in the morning, or just 
support your health without pills, beet juice might just be your game-changer. From improving 
brain oxygen at night to helping your muscles recover faster while you sleep, beet juice works 
quietly in the background while you're tucked in. In this video, I’ll walk you through 8 
powerful, science-supported benefits of drinking just one cup of beet juice before bed. 
Each one targets a different part of your body, and some of them are surprisingly unexpected.
But before you start chugging that deep red juice, remember, always talk to 
your doctor, especially if you’re on medication or have low blood pressure.
And make sure to stick around till the end to find out who should avoid drinking beet juice at night.
Let’s get started. Naturally Lowers Nighttime Blood Pressure
Most people don’t expect their bedtime drink to do anything for their heart, but beet 
juice does something pretty incredible. When you drink it before going to bed, 
it quietly goes to work lowering your blood pressure while you sleep. The first key 
player is naturally occurring dietary nitrate. After you drink beet juice, your body converts 
these nitrates into nitric oxide, a gas molecule that helps relax and widen your blood 
vessels. This process is called vasodilation, and it allows blood to flow more easily, reducing 
the pressure inside your arteries without the need for medication. And the best part? This happens 
gradually and gently throughout the night, syncing perfectly with your body’s 
natural nighttime blood pressure dip. But there’s another hero ingredient at work 
that is potassium. When potassium levels rise, excess sodium gets flushed out more efficiently, 
easing tension in your blood vessel walls. That means less resistance for your blood to push 
against. The combined effect of nitric oxide relaxing your arteries and potassium clearing 
out pressure-raising sodium creates a smooth, natural drop in blood pressure.
A recent study featured in “Frontiers in Physiology” explored what happens when people 
drink beetroot juice loaded with natural nitrates. Participants were split into two groups; 
one received a high-nitrate beet juice, while the other got a placebo version with 
hardly any nitrates. What stood out was the speed of the effect. Within just 30 minutes, 
the group that drank the nitrate-rich juice showed a noticeably sharper drop in blood 
pressure than those in the placebo group. Supports Liver Detox During Nighttime Repair
Drinking one cup of beet juice before bed may actually help your liver do that job 
better, faster, and more efficiently. What makes such an effect possible is how 
beet juice fuels the liver’s natural detox process without overstimulating it. One of 
the key compounds responsible is betaine, a nutrient that supports liver cell function 
and helps your body process fats and toxins. When you drink beet juice, betaine helps the 
liver produce more of a powerful antioxidant called glutathione. That antioxidant 
binds to toxins and waste materials, making them easier for the body to eliminate 
through bile or urine. Since glutathione activity naturally increases overnight, having beet juice 
in your system before sleep means your liver gets extra support right when it needs it most.
Apart from that, we have manganese in beet juice. This trace mineral helps activate 
enzymes involved in breaking down ammonia, alcohol, and other metabolic waste products. 
During deep sleep, your liver ramps up these enzyme-driven pathways to clean your blood, and 
manganese makes sure those processes run smoothly. A 12-week randomized controlled trial, published 
in “Frontiers in Nutrition” and backed by Garmian Polytechnic University, looked at how daily 
beetroot extract could influence liver health. The study followed 180 adults ranging from 19 
to 73 years old. After consistent daily intake, participants showed a noticeable drop in 
key liver markers like serum bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase. These improvements 
point to beetroot juice as a potential ally in supporting liver detoxification 
and maintaining healthy liver function. Boosts Brain Oxygen For Sharper Mornings
Ever wake up feeling foggy, like your brain just hasn’t caught up with the day yet? That 
morning grogginess might actually be linked to how well your brain received oxygen during 
the night. When you drink beet juice, your body converts its natural nitrates into nitric oxide, a 
molecule that relaxes and opens up blood vessels. This wider pathway allows more oxygen-rich 
blood to reach areas like your frontal cortex, the part of your brain responsible for focus, 
decision-making, and alertness. During sleep, especially the deeper stages, the brain’s 
demand for oxygen increases as it clears out waste and resets cognitive function. 
With better blood flow from nitric oxide, this cleanup process becomes more efficient, 
which translates into clearer thinking and quicker mental sharpness when you wake up.
What also plays a crucial role is the presence of iron in beet juice. This mineral 
supports the production of hemoglobin, the protein in red blood cells that carries 
oxygen throughout your body, including your brain. Higher hemoglobin levels mean more oxygen 
gets delivered per heartbeat. That steady, enriched oxygen supply while you sleep helps 
your brain function; it helps it thrive. A study featured by the “National Library 
of Medicine” found something remarkable: older adults between the ages of 68 
and 82 who consumed higher amounts of natural nitrates saw better blood flow to 
the white matter in their frontal lobe. This area of the brain is deeply involved in 
memory, learning, and critical thinking. With improved circulation, brain cells 
receive more oxygen and essential nutrients, creating an environment that supports sharper 
cognitive function and long-term brain health. Enhances Overnight Skin Rejuvenation
You might not consider beet juice to be a beauty drink, but your skin definitely does. 
While you sleep, your body kicks into repair mode, and your skin takes full advantage of 
it. One cup of beet juice before bed may actually speed up that overnight renewal 
process, helping your skin recover from daily stress and wake up looking more refreshed. 
The secret starts with the antioxidants inside beet juice, especially vitamin C.
As highlighted on “PubMed,” beetroot naturally packs a solid dose of vitamin C, an 
essential nutrient your skin can’t do without. Vitamin C fuels the production of collagen, 
the protein that gives skin its firmness, bounce, and smooth texture. When your 
collagen levels are supported, your skin holds onto its youthful structure, helping to 
keep wrinkles, sagging, and dullness at bay. Along with that, it also helps neutralize free 
radicals, unstable molecules that break down skin cells and speed up aging, leaving your 
complexion looking smoother and more even. Moreover, zinc is yet another essential 
nutrient that is beneficial to the skin and may be found in beet juice. It may not 
get as much attention, but zinc is crucial for cell turnover and healing. It supports the 
enzymes your skin uses to rebuild its protective barrier and manage inflammation. While you 
rest, zinc gets to work calming irritation, reducing redness, and promoting fresh, 
healthy skin cells to rise to the surface. Stabilizes Blood Sugar Levels At Night
Blood sugar spikes at night might not wake you up, but they may quietly interrupt your sleep 
cycle and leave you feeling drained in the morning. What’s surprising is how something 
as simple as drinking beet juice before bed may help keep those glucose levels steady.
Even though beets taste naturally sweet, their glycemic load is low, meaning they release 
sugar into your bloodstream slowly and steadily. Alpha-lipoic acid, a potent antioxidant that may 
be found in beet juice, stands out as one of the most important factors that contribute to this 
effect. This compound improves how your cells respond to insulin, the hormone responsible for 
moving sugar out of your blood and into your cells for energy. When insulin works better, your 
body may avoid those late-night sugar spikes and crashes that mess with your rest and recovery.
Magnesium is yet another essential mineral that may be found in beet juice and plays a significant 
role. This isn’t just good for muscles and nerves; it’s also critical for regulating how your body 
processes glucose. Magnesium helps enzymes in your pancreas work more efficiently so they may release 
insulin at the right time and in the right amount. That means fewer disruptions to your sleep, a 
more balanced metabolism, and a better shot at waking up without that sluggish, foggy feeling.
Research published on “PubMed” examined how daily beet consumption could impact blood sugar control 
in people with diabetes. In a study involving 44 diabetic patients, participants ate raw beets each 
day over an eight-week period. By the end of the study, their fasting blood sugar levels dropped 
significantly. These results point to raw beets as a simple, natural way to help regulate glucose 
levels and support better metabolic health. Improves Gut Health And Digestion
It’s easy to forget that your digestive system keeps working even after you go to bed. And 
drinking beet juice before sleep may actually help your gut function better, not just while you're 
awake, but through the entire night. What makes beet juice so effective for digestion is that it 
supports both the physical movement of your gut and the health of your microbiome. One standout 
compound responsible for this is dietary fiber, especially the type that acts as a prebiotic.
Prebiotics are like food for the good bacteria in your gut, and when you feed them right, they 
multiply and help keep harmful bacteria in check. As these healthy microbes thrive, they improve 
how well your gut absorbs nutrients and how efficiently it eliminates waste, which makes your 
digestion feel smoother and lighter the next day. The natural betacyanins that are found 
in beet juice are another benefit that is often overlooked. Betacyanins are the pigments 
that give beet juice its deep red color. These compounds have been shown to reduce inflammation 
in the digestive tract, which is crucial for people dealing with bloating, discomfort, or 
sluggish digestion. When your gut lining is calm and balanced, the muscles in your digestive 
tract contract more rhythmically, pushing food and waste through at a healthier pace. So while 
you’re resting, beet juice is doing the opposite; it’s actively helping your digestive system reset, 
recover, and prepare for a better start tomorrow. A pilot study cited on “PubMed” revealed that 
just two weeks of drinking red beetroot juice sparked noticeable shifts in the gut microbiome 
of healthy adults. Researchers linked these changes to betacyanins, the vibrant pigments 
that give beets their deep red color. These compounds appeared to boost levels of beneficial 
bacteria like Bifidobacterium while dialing down Ruminococcus, a group tied to gut imbalances. The 
results point to beet juice as a natural way to support a healthier digestive system, strengthen 
immunity, and promote overall gut wellness. Reduces Inflammation And Oxidative Stress
You might not see it happening, but low-grade inflammation and oxidative stress are constantly 
at work in the background, slowing your recovery, aging your cells, and draining your energy. 
That’s why drinking just one cup of beet juice before bed may be a smart move. While you’re 
asleep, your body is already in repair mode, and beet juice helps speed up that healing by 
targeting the root causes of cellular damage. A pigment known as betanin happens to be one of 
the most potent compounds that are responsible. This natural antioxidant fights off harmful 
free radicals, unstable molecules that build up from stress, pollution, poor diet, or 
even a tough workout. When betanin binds to these free radicals, it neutralizes 
them before they may damage tissues, keeping your cells healthier and your body 
more resilient. This process not only reduces wear and tear on your organs but also helps 
slow down signs of aging from the inside out. What also plays a critical role here is the 
presence of vitamin C in beet juice. Known for its immune-boosting powers, vitamin C also acts as an 
anti-inflammatory agent by calming down the immune response when it gets too aggressive. At night, 
your immune system shifts into a regulation mode, clearing out damaged cells and resetting 
your internal balance. Vitamin C helps guide that process, making it more efficient 
without triggering excessive inflammation. Betalains, in particular betacyanins, have 
been shown to reduce inflammation in cells, according to a study published in "PubMed." 
This evidence indicates that betacyanins have the ability to reduce inflammation 
as well as antioxidant properties. Strengthens Cardiovascular Function During Sleep
Most people consider heart health to be something you work on during the day through exercise, diet, 
or stress management. But drinking one cup of beet juice before bed may help support that process in 
a powerful way. Folate, also known as vitamin B9, is one of the most important nutrients responsible 
for this. It supports the production of red blood cells and helps lower homocysteine levels, a 
compound in your blood that, when elevated, may increase the risk of heart disease. By keeping 
homocysteine in check and supporting blood cell health, folate allows your heart and vessels 
to recover more efficiently while you rest. Along with that, when the nitrates in beet 
juice are converted into nitric oxide, they help your arteries stay flexible 
and relaxed, making it easier for your heart to pump blood with less effort.
This benefit becomes especially important at night, when your heart rate slows and your body 
prioritizes maintenance over activity. With better blood vessel function and improved circulation, 
your heart may distribute oxygen and nutrients more effectively throughout the body. So while 
you’re asleep, folate is optimizing the internal chemistry that keeps your heart strong, and nitric 
oxide is making the physical workload easier on your cardiovascular system. And the result? Well, 
a heart that is more efficient and healthier, and that awakens with the same level of energy as you.
According to“ PubMed,” a 12-week study tracked 180 participants aged 19 to 73 who followed one 
of three diets: a standard diet, one enriched with beetroot juice, and another combining 
beetroot juice with a Mediterranean-style eating plan. Blood test results revealed that 
those who consumed beetroot juice, especially when paired with the Mediterranean diet, saw 
notable improvements in their cholesterol profile. They experienced lower levels of LDL (bad 
cholesterol) and a rise in HDL (good cholesterol), both of which play a key role in strengthening 
heart health and reducing cardiovascular risk. Who Should Not Drink Beet Juice Before Bed?
Beet juice may be a game-changer for a lot of us, but it’s not for everyone. I know it sounds like 
a natural, harmless drink, and for most people, it is. But if you have certain health conditions 
or take specific medications, beet juice could actually do more harm than good. Before you jump 
in and add it to your nightly routine, you need to know if you fall into any of these categories.
1. People With Low Blood Pressure (Hypotension) Beet juice naturally lowers blood pressure, 
which is great for most, but not if yours is already too low. It may cause dizziness, 
fainting, or even a dangerous drop overnight. 2. Those Prone To Kidney Stones
Beets are high in oxalates, which may contribute to kidney stone formation. 
If you’ve had stones in the past or are prone to them, it’s best to avoid beet juice.
3. Individuals With Chronic Kidney Disease High potassium in beet juice may be 
problematic if your kidneys can’t filter it out properly. This could lead to 
dangerous potassium buildup in your blood. 4. Anyone Taking Blood Pressure Medication
If you're already on antihypertensive drugs, beet juice might intensify their effect and 
cause your blood pressure to drop too low. Always check with your doctor.
5. People With Iron Overload Disorders (e.g., Hemochromatosis)
Beet juice is rich in iron and could worsen symptoms for people who already 
have high iron levels in their blood. 6. Those With Sensitive Stomachs Or IBS
Some people find beet juice may trigger bloating, gas, or stomach cramps. If you have a 
sensitive gut or deal with irritable bowel issues, start slow or avoid it altogether.
7. Pregnant Women With Gestational Complications Beet juice may affect blood 
pressure and potassium levels, which may be tricky during pregnancy. If 
you're expecting and have any complications, don’t drink it without your doctor’s OK.
8. People Undergoing Surgery Soon Because beet juice affects blood pressure and 
blood flow, it may interfere with anesthesia or post-op recovery. Doctors usually recommend 
stopping it at least two weeks before surgery. 9. Anyone With A Known Allergy To Beets
Though rare, beet allergies do exist. If you’ve ever had itching, hives, or a strange reaction 
after eating beets, skip the juice entirely. 10. Those Who Experience Beeturia And Panic
If you see red or pink in your urine or stool after drinking beet juice, it’s probably 
harmless beeturia. But if it causes unnecessary worry or overlaps with other 
symptoms, it may not be worth the stress. Just one cup of beet juice before bed isn’t 
some quick fix; it actually sets you up for a full-body reset while you sleep. 
When I started drinking it at night, I had no idea it could quietly work on so many 
things at once. It helps your circulation, supports your liver’s detox process, boosts muscle 
recovery, and even gives your brain a better chance to recharge. It’s like giving your body a 
head start on healing before the next day begins. If you’ve been looking for a simple way to 
sleep deeper, wake up clearer, or even just feel better in your own skin, this deep red drink might 
surprise you. I’ve felt the difference myself, and now it’s your turn to see what happens when you 
make beet juice a part of your nighttime routine. Now that we have discussed the many benefits 
of drinking one cup of beet juice before bed, let’s move on to other topics. Are you interested 
in learning more about your body? Watch: 13 SUPER Foods Every Diabetic 
Should Be Eating TODAY! Or watch 9 POWERFUL Health 
Benefits Of Eating OATS Every Day Watch one, or even better, watch both. Do you drink beet juice before bed? Let 
us know in the comments below.

diabetic patients, participants ate raw beets each 
day over an eight-week period. By the end of the

study, their fasting blood sugar levels dropped 
significantly. These results point to raw beets

as a simple, natural way to help regulate glucose 
levels and support better metabolic health.

Improves Gut Health And Digestion
It’s easy to forget that your digestive

system keeps working even after you go to bed. And 
drinking beet juice before sleep may actually help

your gut function better, not just while you're 
awake, but through the entire night. What makes

beet juice so effective for digestion is that it 
supports both the physical movement of your gut

and the health of your microbiome. One standout 
compound responsible for this is dietary fiber,

especially the type that acts as a prebiotic.
Prebiotics are like food for the good bacteria

in your gut, and when you feed them right, they 
multiply and help keep harmful bacteria in check.

As these healthy microbes thrive, they improve 
how well your gut absorbs nutrients and how

efficiently it eliminates waste, which makes your 
digestion feel smoother and lighter the next day.

The natural betacyanins that are found 
in beet juice are another benefit that is

often overlooked. Betacyanins are the pigments 
that give beet juice its deep red color. These

compounds have been shown to reduce inflammation 
in the digestive tract, which is crucial for

people dealing with bloating, discomfort, or 
sluggish digestion. When your gut lining is

calm and balanced, the muscles in your digestive 
tract contract more rhythmically, pushing food

and waste through at a healthier pace. So while 
you’re resting, beet juice is doing the opposite;

it’s actively helping your digestive system reset, 
recover, and prepare for a better start tomorrow.

A pilot study cited on “PubMed” revealed that 
just two weeks of drinking red beetroot juice

sparked noticeable shifts in the gut microbiome 
of healthy adults. Researchers linked these

changes to betacyanins, the vibrant pigments 
that give beets their deep red color. These

compounds appeared to boost levels of beneficial 
bacteria like Bifidobacterium while dialing down

Ruminococcus, a group tied to gut imbalances. The 
results point to beet juice as a natural way to

support a healthier digestive system, strengthen 
immunity, and promote overall gut wellness.

Reduces Inflammation And Oxidative Stress
You might not see it happening, but low-grade

inflammation and oxidative stress are constantly 
at work in the background, slowing your recovery,

aging your cells, and draining your energy. 
That’s why drinking just one cup of beet juice

before bed may be a smart move. While you’re 
asleep, your body is already in repair mode,

and beet juice helps speed up that healing by 
targeting the root causes of cellular damage.

A pigment known as betanin happens to be one of 
the most potent compounds that are responsible.

This natural antioxidant fights off harmful 
free radicals, unstable molecules that build

up from stress, pollution, poor diet, or 
even a tough workout. When betanin binds

to these free radicals, it neutralizes 
them before they may damage tissues,

keeping your cells healthier and your body 
more resilient. This process not only reduces

wear and tear on your organs but also helps 
slow down signs of aging from the inside out.

What also plays a critical role here is the 
presence of vitamin C in beet juice. Known for its

immune-boosting powers, vitamin C also acts as an 
anti-inflammatory agent by calming down the immune

response when it gets too aggressive. At night, 
your immune system shifts into a regulation mode,

clearing out damaged cells and resetting 
your internal balance. Vitamin C helps

guide that process, making it more efficient 
without triggering excessive inflammation.

Betalains, in particular betacyanins, have 
been shown to reduce inflammation in cells,

according to a study published in "PubMed." 
This evidence indicates that betacyanins have

the ability to reduce inflammation 
as well as antioxidant properties.

Strengthens Cardiovascular Function During Sleep
Most people consider heart health to be something

you work on during the day through exercise, diet, 
or stress management. But drinking one cup of beet

juice before bed may help support that process in 
a powerful way. Folate, also known as vitamin B9,

is one of the most important nutrients responsible 
for this. It supports the production of red blood

cells and helps lower homocysteine levels, a 
compound in your blood that, when elevated,

may increase the risk of heart disease. By keeping 
homocysteine in check and supporting blood cell

health, folate allows your heart and vessels 
to recover more efficiently while you rest.

Along with that, when the nitrates in beet 
juice are converted into nitric oxide,

they help your arteries stay flexible 
and relaxed, making it easier for your

heart to pump blood with less effort.
This benefit becomes especially important

at night, when your heart rate slows and your body 
prioritizes maintenance over activity. With better

blood vessel function and improved circulation, 
your heart may distribute oxygen and nutrients

more effectively throughout the body. So while 
you’re asleep, folate is optimizing the internal

chemistry that keeps your heart strong, and nitric 
oxide is making the physical workload easier on

your cardiovascular system. And the result? Well, 
a heart that is more efficient and healthier, and

that awakens with the same level of energy as you.
According to“ PubMed,” a 12-week study tracked 180

participants aged 19 to 73 who followed one 
of three diets: a standard diet, one enriched

with beetroot juice, and another combining 
beetroot juice with a Mediterranean-style

eating plan. Blood test results revealed that 
those who consumed beetroot juice, especially

when paired with the Mediterranean diet, saw 
notable improvements in their cholesterol

profile. They experienced lower levels of LDL (bad 
cholesterol) and a rise in HDL (good cholesterol),

both of which play a key role in strengthening 
heart health and reducing cardiovascular risk.

Who Should Not Drink Beet Juice Before Bed?
Beet juice may be a game-changer for a lot of us,

but it’s not for everyone. I know it sounds like 
a natural, harmless drink, and for most people,

it is. But if you have certain health conditions 
or take specific medications, beet juice could

actually do more harm than good. Before you jump 
in and add it to your nightly routine, you need

to know if you fall into any of these categories.
1. People With Low Blood Pressure (Hypotension)

Beet juice naturally lowers blood pressure, 
which is great for most, but not if yours

is already too low. It may cause dizziness, 
fainting, or even a dangerous drop overnight.

2. Those Prone To Kidney Stones
Beets are high in oxalates,

which may contribute to kidney stone formation. 
If you’ve had stones in the past or are prone

to them, it’s best to avoid beet juice.
3. Individuals With Chronic Kidney Disease

High potassium in beet juice may be 
problematic if your kidneys can’t

filter it out properly. This could lead to 
dangerous potassium buildup in your blood.

4. Anyone Taking Blood Pressure Medication
If you're already on antihypertensive drugs,

beet juice might intensify their effect and 
cause your blood pressure to drop too low.

Always check with your doctor.
5. People With Iron Overload

Disorders (e.g., Hemochromatosis)
Beet juice is rich in iron and could

worsen symptoms for people who already 
have high iron levels in their blood.

6. Those With Sensitive Stomachs Or IBS
Some people find beet juice may trigger bloating,

gas, or stomach cramps. If you have a 
sensitive gut or deal with irritable

bowel issues, start slow or avoid it altogether.
7. Pregnant Women With Gestational Complications

Beet juice may affect blood 
pressure and potassium levels,

which may be tricky during pregnancy. If 
you're expecting and have any complications,

don’t drink it without your doctor’s OK.
8. People Undergoing Surgery Soon

Because beet juice affects blood pressure and 
blood flow, it may interfere with anesthesia

or post-op recovery. Doctors usually recommend 
stopping it at least two weeks before surgery.

9. Anyone With A Known Allergy To Beets
Though rare, beet allergies do exist. If you’ve

ever had itching, hives, or a strange reaction 
after eating beets, skip the juice entirely.

10. Those Who Experience Beeturia And Panic
If you see red or pink in your urine or stool

after drinking beet juice, it’s probably 
harmless beeturia. But if it causes

unnecessary worry or overlaps with other 
symptoms, it may not be worth the stress.

Just one cup of beet juice before bed isn’t 
some quick fix; it actually sets you up for

a full-body reset while you sleep. 
When I started drinking it at night,

I had no idea it could quietly work on so many 
things at once. It helps your circulation,

supports your liver’s detox process, boosts muscle 
recovery, and even gives your brain a better

chance to recharge. It’s like giving your body a 
head start on healing before the next day begins.

If you’ve been looking for a simple way to 
sleep deeper, wake up clearer, or even just feel

better in your own skin, this deep red drink might 
surprise you. I’ve felt the difference myself, and

now it’s your turn to see what happens when you 
make beet juice a part of your nighttime routine.

Now that we have discussed the many benefits 
of drinking one cup of beet juice before bed,

let’s move on to other topics. Are you interested 
in learning more about your body? Watch:

13 SUPER Foods Every Diabetic 
Should Be Eating TODAY!

Or watch 9 POWERFUL Health 
Benefits Of Eating OATS Every Day
Watch one, or even better, watch both. Do you

drink beet juice before bed? Let 
us know in the comments below.

videos.title: Over 60? NEVER Drink Beetroot Juice With These 7 Foods | Senior Vitality

transcript.fullText: For more than three decades of research at Harvard, heart specialists have discovered something that forced many doctors to rethink all their nutrition advice for people over 60. They found that after the age of 60, the body loses nearly 70% of its ability to produce nitric oxide, the molecule that widens blood vessels and delivers oxygen to the heart, brain, and legs. This is exactly why beet juice is considered a natural medicine for circulation. However, during clinical discussions, researchers began to notice a paradox. More and more patients were drinking beet juice, yet still feeling dizzy, bloated, having stomach pain, and even increasing their risk of kidney stones. When they reviewed the food diaries, the answer became very clear. I remember one case at Mayo Clinic. Judith, 72 years old, started drinking beet juice every morning on a friend's recommendation. But after only two weeks, she had severe stomach cramps, low blood pressure, and had to be hospitalized. The problem was not the beets themselves. It was that she had accidentally combined beets with a food that seemed harmless. This is something almost no one is warned about. Beets are very powerful, and when combined the wrong way, they can react in ways older adults never expect. This goes completely against the popular belief that anything healthy must be good. But science shows a very different truth. Some foods cause strong fermentation in the gut. Some disrupt nitrates. Some increase oxalates to levels the kidneys cannot handle. And some make blood pressure swing in dangerous ways. The scariest part is that these foods are all familiar. And they are even promoted in juice recipes widely shared online. In this video, we will explore seven foods you should never combine with beet juice, including one that is at the very top of the list and is still used everyday by many people over 60 without realizing the risk. If this is your first time here, please subscribe to the channel. We share health tips designed especially for older adults that you will rarely find anywhere else. Now, let us begin the journey of protecting your health, starting with the very first glass of juice you drink each morning. Food number one, fresh ginger. Ginger is one of the most loved foods among older adults because it warms the body, supports digestion, and acts as a natural pain reliever. But this is also the exact reason it becomes one of the most dangerous foods to combine with beet juice, especially for anyone over 60. What most people do not realize is that both ginger and beets act strongly on the circulatory system. And when the two meet, the body can become overloaded in a very real biological sense. Many studies at the University of Maryland and at integrated medicine centers across the United States show that ginger contains gingerol and shogal, two compounds that thin the blood and increase circulation. In younger people, this can be a benefit. But in older adults whose blood vessel walls are already thinner and whose blood pressure fluctuates more easily, strong ginger can make blood pressure drop quickly within just a few minutes. When this is combined with beetroot, which widens blood vessels by increasing natural nitric oxide by up to 30%. This double pull on blood pressure becomes a problem. It can make many people feel dizzy, weak in the legs, or as if they are about to faint. One 72-year-old patient at a clinic in Minnesota shared that he started drinking beet juice every morning to improve circulation. Thinking ginger would be good for his stomach, he added a small piece. After only 3 days, he stood up from his chair and became so laded that he had to hold onto the wall to avoid falling. When doctors checked him, they confirmed that his blood pressure was dropping suddenly every morning after drinking this mixture. It is not only blood pressure that is affected. Ginger also strongly stimulates bowel movements. For people with irritable bowel syndrome, sensitive stomachs, or frequent mild diarrhea, adding ginger to beet juice can cause cramping, repeated trips to the toilet, and a burning sensation in the stomach. Research at Mayo Clinic shows that people over 60 have more than 30% less ability to regulate stomach acid, which makes them react more strongly to stimulating foods like ginger. Especially if you are taking blood thinners such as warerin, eloquis or daily aspirin, ginger becomes even more dangerous. Both ginger and beets increase blood flow. When combined with these medications, the effect can become excessive and may lead to bruising, nose bleeds, or dangerously low blood pressure. If you like a warm, slightly spicy flavor, replace ginger with mint, cucumber, or green apple. These [snorts] choices still make the juice easier to drink without harming circulation or digestion. Older adults can absolutely use ginger, but it must be used correctly and it should never be combined with beet juice. Food number two, vinegar. This is one of the most misleading combinations for older adults. Many detox recipes online encourage mixing apple cider vinegar with beet juice to boost cleansing effects. But the truth is completely the opposite. When you add vinegar to beet juice, you are weakening one of the most important biological processes for circulation in people over 60. Vinegar, especially apple cider vinegar, contains concentrated acetic acid. This acid can change the pH environment in the stomach and intestines within minutes. When this acid comes into contact with nitrates and beets, which are the compounds that help the body produce nitric oxide, it reduces nitrate stability and interferes with the physiological conversion that the body is trying to perform. Nitric oxide is a powerful vessel widening molecule. But when nitrates are broken down before absorption, this effect is almost completely lost. A study from Cleveland Clinic found that acetic acid in vinegar can reduce the stability of nitrates in the digestive environment by up to 40%. In people over 60, absorption and metabolism are already reduced naturally with age. So this effect is even stronger. As a result, many people drink beet juice with vinegar every morning and do not feel any real improvement in circulation. Some even feel dizzy, weak in the legs, or shaky when standing up because their blood pressure becomes unstable. On top of that, vinegar directly affects the stomach lining. After age 60, the protective layer of the stomach becomes thinner, and mucus production drops by nearly 30%. This makes the acid in vinegar more likely to cause burning, tightness in the upper abdomen, acid reflux, and sometimes a dull ongoing pain after drinking. Some patients with a history of ulcers or reflux react strongly even to just one or two tablespoons of vinegar. What few people know is that vinegar also affects electrolyte balance, especially potassium. When it is combined with beetroot, which already has the ability to lower blood pressure, this shift in electrolytes can create a double drop effect on blood pressure. Older adults may feel laded, extremely tired, or as if their vision darkens when they stand up from a chair. In short, vinegar can be helpful when used correctly, for example, in salads or cooking. But when mixed with beet juice, it removes nitrate benefits, irritates the digestive tract, and can make blood pressure swings more dangerous. If you want a refreshing feel in your beet juice, choose cucumber or celery instead. They are gentler, safer, and much friendlier to the bodies of older adults. Food number three, lemon. Lemon is the symbol of fresh detox and fat burning. And almost everyone who pursues a healthy lifestyle believes that adding lemon to any juice will increase its benefits. However, for beet juice, especially for people over 60, lemon is a very common mistake that can cause several unwanted effects. The problem is not lemon itself or beetroot itself. It is how their chemical properties interact with each other. Beetroot contains betylanes, active nitrates, and a group of pigments that are very sensitive to changes in pH. When you add a large amount of lemon to be juice, the environment becomes strongly acidic, which makes many of the compounds in beets less stable. A study from the Harvard School of Public Health noted that a strongly acidic environment can reduce the bioavailability of certain forms of nitrates, making it harder for the body to convert them into nitric oxide. In younger people, this reduction may not matter much. But in older adults whose absorption is already lower, the effect becomes more noticeable. Beyond the nitrate issue, lemon directly affects the digestive system. After age 60, the stomach loses about 30 to 40% of its ability to buffer acid. This makes the stomach lining more sensitive to strongly acidic foods. When beet juice, which already gently stimulates bowel movement, is combined with lemon, many people experience burning in the stomach, pain or pressure in the upper abdomen, or persistent acid reflux. A small survey at Mayo Clinic on patients with reflux found that more than 60% reacted strongly when they consumed acidic juices together with nitraterich foods. Another issue that many people overlook is that lemon can cause mild lightadedness in older adults. When acid stimulates the stomach, the body responds by increasing stomach secretions, which can sometimes lead to a temporary drop in blood sugar or small shifts in blood pressure. If this happens together with beet juice, which already widens blood vessels, the combined effect can make some people feel weak, shaky, or as if their vision darkens when they stand up. This does not mean you must remove lemon from your life. Lemon has many wonderful benefits. But when it comes to beet juice, the key to safety is moderation. If you enjoy a slight sour taste, use only a few drops and never half a lemon or a whole lemon as many online recipes suggest. And if you have acid reflux, ulcers, or a sensitive stomach, it is best to skip lemon completely when you drink beet juice. The most important thing for older adults to remember is this. Beetroot works best in a neutral or slightly alkaline environment. The more you interfere with strong acids, the more the benefits go down and the risks go up. Food number four, carrots. Hook. Many people think that combining one root vegetable with another root vegetable must be even better and more nutritious. But with beetroot and carrots, this seemingly harmless combination can silently burden the kidneys, blood sugar, and circulation of people over 60. Carrots are famous for being rich in beta carotene, vitamin A, and antioxidants. But what most people do not know is that carrots also belong to a group of foods with medium to high oxalate levels. When they are juiced, meaning most of the fiber is removed, oxalates are absorbed much more strongly than when they are eaten whole. Beetroot, meanwhile, is in the very high oxalate group. This combination creates a double oxalate dose that forces the kidneys of older adults to work much harder. Studies published in the Journal of Urology show that people over 60 have almost 50% less ability to excrete oxalates through the kidneys compared with younger adults. This means that with the same amount of oxalates, older people are more likely to accumulate calcium oxalate crystals, the main component of more than 70% of kidney stones. These crystals can lead to kidney stones, lower back pain, burning when urinating, or pain while passing urine. Another clinical survey from the National Kidney Foundation found that high oxalate juices, especially beet and carrot mixtures, are linked to a higher rate of urinary discomfort in people over 60. It is not just oxalates. Carrot juice also releases a fairly high amount of natural sugars. Without fiber, glucose and fructose enter the bloodstream much faster, causing a sharp jump in blood sugar. In people with pre-diabetes, type 2 diabetes, or age- related insulin resistance, the carrot and beet combination can cause fatigue, shaky hands, and sleepiness after drinking. Research from Mayo Clinic shows that carrot juice raises blood sugar levels up to three times faster than boiled or whole carrots. A typical case is Eleanor, 68 years old, who was generally healthy except for mild joint pain. After hearing about a beet carrot detox recipe online, she drank it every morning for 2 weeks. By day 14, she began feeling burning when she urinated and a dull ache in her hip area. Her urine test showed the presence of oxalate crystals, a sign that her kidneys were trying to handle more waste than they could safely process. This does not mean carrots are bad. Like any food, the key is how you use them. Whole carrots, cooked carrots, or carrots combined with low oxilate vegetables are completely fine. But when it comes to beet juice, this is a pair that people over 60 should avoid. Food number five, sugar or honey. Many older adults feel that beet juice tastes earthy and a bit hard to drink, so they often add a spoon of honey or a little sugar to make it more pleasant. It sounds harmless and is even seen as a natural sweetness. But in real physiology, adding sugar, whether white sugar, brown sugar, or honey, is one of the mistakes that wipes out most of the benefits of beets and can harm heart health in people over 60. Beetroot is rich in natural nitrates, which the body converts into nitric oxide, a molecule that widens blood vessels, improves circulation, and lowers blood pressure. But when sugar is added at the same time, [snorts] studies from Mayo Clinic show that the sudden insulin surge can reduce the sensitivity of nitric oxide receptors in the blood vessel walls. This means that even though nitrates are still absorbed, the body does not respond as effectively. Another clinical report from the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition found that high sugar intake in a meal can reduce nitric oxide activity by up to 30% within 45 minutes. For older adults, the problem is even more obvious. After 60, the body has more difficulty regulating blood sugar, and sugar is absorbed into the bloodstream more quickly because the protective mucous layer of the intestines becomes thinner. When sugar or honey is added to beet juice, which already has no fiber, the sugar rushes into the blood, causing blood sugar to spike and then drop quickly. This creates feelings of exhaustion, shaky hands, headaches, and sleepiness after drinking. For people with pre-diabetes or type 2 diabetes, these swings are especially dangerous. Beyond weakening nitric oxide benefits, sugar also triggers whole body inflammation. A study from Harvard found that just one spoon of simple sugar can raise the inflammatory marker CRP by up to 18% within 1 hour. Inflammation damages the inner lining of blood vessels, the very part that beetroot is trying to support. In other words, by adding a little sugar, you are stepping on the brakes of the circulatory benefits that beets are trying to provide. Honey, although natural, is still sugar at the biochemical level. It raises blood sugar in a way that is very similar to white sugar and still causes strong insulin responses. Some people believe honey is healthier, but this is only true when small amounts are used in meals that are high in fiber, not when honey is mixed into beet juice that has had its fiber removed. If you need a little sweetness, use a small amount of green apple or pear. These fruits have a lower glycemic index and do not disturb nitrate activity. But if you want beetroot to deliver its full benefits, do not add sugar or honey. Food number six, spinach. Hook. This is a leafy green praised across the United States as a superfood, but when combined with beet juice, it can become one of the silent threats to the kidneys of people over 60. Spinach is rich in iron, folate, vitamin K, and many strong antioxidants. But its biggest limitation lies in its extremely high oxalate content. Oxalates can bind with calcium to form calcium oxalate crystals which make up more than 70% of kidney stones in the United States. According to the National Kidney Foundation, beetroot is also a very high oxalate food. When spinach and beetroot are juiced together, the total oxalate load does not just add up. It becomes much easier to absorb because juicing removes most of the fiber that would normally help limit oxalate absorption in the gut. Research from the National Institutes of Health shows that people over 60 have about 50% less ability to excrete oxalates through the kidneys than younger adults. This means that from a single glass of juice, the oxalate overload in an older person is effectively doubled. When oxalates go beyond what the body can safely handle, they begin to silently crystallize in the kidneys or urinary tract. Early symptoms are often very mild, such as vague lower back pain, slight burning during urination, cloudy urine, or small visible particles. Many people mistake this for dehydration or simple fatigue from aging. An observational study at Mayo Clinic found that more than 60% of older patients with small kidney stones regularly drank high oxilate juices, especially mixtures of spinach and beetroot. High oxalate levels do more than affect the kidneys. They can also lock up minerals like calcium and iron, making them harder to absorb. For older adults who are already at risk of iron deficiency anemia, this is concerning. Some patients report ongoing fatigue, weakness in the arms and legs, and slow recovery after activity. These can be signs that mineral absorption is being disrupted. Spinach is still a good food, but again, the way you use it matters. Older adults can safely eat cooked spinach or spinach served with high-fiber meals because cooking significantly reduces oxalate content, but they should avoid juicing raw spinach together with beets. If you want to combine beets with leafy greens, choose romaine lettuce, kale, or celery. These are low oxilate vegetables and are much friendlier to the kidneys of older adults. Food number seven, oranges. Oranges are a popular breakfast fruit among older Americans because they are rich in vitamin C, have a pleasant taste, and feel healthy. However, when combined with beet juice, especially in pure juice form, oranges can trigger biological reactions that most people over 60 never anticipate. The issue lies in the interaction between acid, natural sugars, and the nitrates unique to beets. Oranges are highly acidic. When citric acid mixes with beet juice, the digestive system must handle two strong stimuli at once, strong acid and active nitrates. In younger people, this combination may not cause noticeable problems. But in older adults whose digestive function and stomach emptying are slower, this mix easily leads to fermentation in the gut. A study published in the journal of gastroenterenterology shows that people over 60 are more likely to experience fermentation, bloating, a type of gut fermentation that causes gas, fullness, heartburn, cramps, and abdominal distension when they consume juices that are high in both acid and simple sugars at the same time. Beyond digestive discomfort, oranges also affect how the body converts beet nitrates. High doses of vitamin C act as a strong reducing agent and under certain conditions they can compete with nitrates during absorption. This makes the conversion of nitrates into nitric oxide less efficient. Research from the University of California shows that when high amounts of vitamin C are consumed in the same meal as nitrates, the nitric oxide levels measured in the blood can drop by 10 to 30%. Weakening the vessel widening benefits of beets. A third problem is the natural sugar in oranges. When juiced, oranges lose all their fiber and fructose enters the bloodstream much faster than when the fruit is eaten whole. In older adults with pre-diabetes or type 2 diabetes, the orange and beet combination can cause sudden blood sugar spikes followed by fatigue, sleepiness, thirst, and sometimes shaky hands or lightadedness. Some patients at Mayo Clinic reported feeling heavy and bloated soon after drinking beet orange juice recipes they found online. When examined, there were no serious issues other than the fact that their digestive systems were reacting strongly to the combination of high acid, high sugar, and nitrates, a triangle of stimulation that the bodies of people over 60 cannot handle as smoothly as before. This does not mean you have to eliminate oranges from your diet. Oranges are still an excellent source of vitamin C when eaten in segments or paired with a proteinrich breakfast, but when it comes to beet juice, avoid mixing them directly. If you like a mild, tangy flavor, golden kiwi or green apple is a safer choice for older adults. How to drink beet juice the right way. Now that you know the seven foods you should never combine with beet juice, the biggest question many people have is this. segments.text: For more than three decades of research
at Harvard, heart specialists have
discovered something that forced many
doctors to rethink all their nutrition
advice for people over 60. They found
that after the age of 60, the body loses
nearly 70% of its ability to produce
nitric oxide, the molecule that widens
blood vessels and delivers oxygen to the
heart, brain, and legs. This is exactly
why beet juice is considered a natural
medicine for circulation. However,
during clinical discussions, researchers
began to notice a paradox. More and more
patients were drinking beet juice, yet
still feeling dizzy, bloated, having
stomach pain, and even increasing their
risk of kidney stones. When they
reviewed the food diaries, the answer
became very clear. I remember one case
at Mayo Clinic. Judith, 72 years old,
started drinking beet juice every
morning on a friend's recommendation.
But after only two weeks, she had severe
stomach cramps, low blood pressure, and
had to be hospitalized. The problem was
not the beets themselves. It was that
she had accidentally combined beets with
a food that seemed harmless. This is
something almost no one is warned about.
Beets are very powerful, and when
combined the wrong way, they can react
in ways older adults never expect. This
goes completely against the popular
belief that anything healthy must be
good. But science shows a very different
truth. Some foods cause strong
fermentation in the gut. Some disrupt
nitrates. Some increase oxalates to
levels the kidneys cannot handle. And
some make blood pressure swing in
dangerous ways. The scariest part is
that these foods are all familiar. And
they are even promoted in juice recipes
widely shared online. In this video, we
will explore seven foods you should
never combine with beet juice, including
one that is at the very top of the list
and is still used everyday by many
people over 60 without realizing the
risk. If this is your first time here,
please subscribe to the channel. We
share health tips designed especially
for older adults that you will rarely
find anywhere else. Now, let us begin
the journey of protecting your health,
starting with the very first glass of
juice you drink each morning.
Food number one, fresh ginger. Ginger is
one of the most loved foods among older
adults because it warms the body,
supports digestion, and acts as a
natural pain reliever. But this is also
the exact reason it becomes one of the
most dangerous foods to combine with
beet juice, especially for anyone over
60. What most people do not realize is
that both ginger and beets act strongly
on the circulatory system. And when the
two meet, the body can become overloaded
in a very real biological sense. Many
studies at the University of Maryland
and at integrated medicine centers
across the United States show that
ginger contains gingerol and shogal, two
compounds that thin the blood and
increase circulation. In younger people,
this can be a benefit. But in older
adults whose blood vessel walls are
already thinner and whose blood pressure
fluctuates more easily, strong ginger
can make blood pressure drop quickly
within just a few minutes. When this is
combined with beetroot, which widens
blood vessels by increasing natural
nitric oxide by up to 30%. This double
pull on blood pressure becomes a
problem. It can make many people feel
dizzy, weak in the legs, or as if they
are about to faint. One 72-year-old
patient at a clinic in Minnesota shared
that he started drinking beet juice
every morning to improve circulation.
Thinking ginger would be good for his
stomach, he added a small piece. After
only 3 days, he stood up from his chair
and became so laded that he had to hold
onto the wall to avoid falling. When
doctors checked him, they confirmed that
his blood pressure was dropping suddenly
every morning after drinking this
mixture. It is not only blood pressure
that is affected. Ginger also strongly
stimulates bowel movements. For people
with irritable bowel syndrome, sensitive
stomachs, or frequent mild diarrhea,
adding ginger to beet juice can cause
cramping, repeated trips to the toilet,
and a burning sensation in the stomach.
Research at Mayo Clinic shows that
people over 60 have more than 30% less
ability to regulate stomach acid, which
makes them react more strongly to
stimulating foods like ginger.
Especially if you are taking blood
thinners such as warerin, eloquis or
daily aspirin, ginger becomes even more
dangerous. Both ginger and beets
increase blood flow. When combined with
these medications, the effect can become
excessive and may lead to bruising, nose
bleeds, or dangerously low blood
pressure. If you like a warm, slightly
spicy flavor, replace ginger with mint,
cucumber, or green apple. These [snorts]
choices still make the juice easier to
drink without harming circulation or
digestion. Older adults can absolutely
use ginger, but it must be used
correctly and it should never be
combined with beet juice. Food number
two, vinegar.
This is one of the most misleading
combinations for older adults. Many
detox recipes online encourage mixing
apple cider vinegar with beet juice to
boost cleansing effects. But the truth
is completely the opposite. When you add
vinegar to beet juice, you are weakening
one of the most important biological
processes for circulation in people over
60. Vinegar, especially apple cider
vinegar, contains concentrated acetic
acid. This acid can change the pH
environment in the stomach and
intestines within minutes. When this
acid comes into contact with nitrates
and beets, which are the compounds that
help the body produce nitric oxide, it
reduces nitrate stability and interferes
with the physiological conversion that
the body is trying to perform. Nitric
oxide is a powerful vessel widening
molecule. But when nitrates are broken
down before absorption, this effect is
almost completely lost. A study from
Cleveland Clinic found that acetic acid
in vinegar can reduce the stability of
nitrates in the digestive environment by
up to 40%. In people over 60, absorption
and metabolism are already reduced
naturally with age. So this effect is
even stronger. As a result, many people
drink beet juice with vinegar every
morning and do not feel any real
improvement in circulation. Some even
feel dizzy, weak in the legs, or shaky
when standing up because their blood
pressure becomes unstable. On top of
that, vinegar directly affects the
stomach lining. After age 60, the
protective layer of the stomach becomes
thinner, and mucus production drops by
nearly 30%. This makes the acid in
vinegar more likely to cause burning,
tightness in the upper abdomen, acid
reflux, and sometimes a dull ongoing
pain after drinking. Some patients with
a history of ulcers or reflux react
strongly even to just one or two
tablespoons of vinegar. What few people
know is that vinegar also affects
electrolyte balance, especially
potassium. When it is combined with
beetroot, which already has the ability
to lower blood pressure, this shift in
electrolytes can create a double drop
effect on blood pressure. Older adults
may feel laded, extremely tired, or as
if their vision darkens when they stand
up from a chair. In short, vinegar can
be helpful when used correctly, for
example, in salads or cooking. But when
mixed with beet juice, it removes
nitrate benefits, irritates the
digestive tract, and can make blood
pressure swings more dangerous. If you
want a refreshing feel in your beet
juice, choose cucumber or celery
instead. They are gentler, safer, and
much friendlier to the bodies of older
adults.
Food number three, lemon. Lemon is the
symbol of fresh detox and fat burning.

healthy lifestyle believes that adding
lemon to any juice will increase its
benefits. However, for beet juice,
especially for people over 60, lemon is
a very common mistake that can cause
several unwanted effects. The problem is
not lemon itself or beetroot itself. It
is how their chemical properties
interact with each other. Beetroot
contains betylanes, active nitrates, and
a group of pigments that are very
sensitive to changes in pH. When you add
a large amount of lemon to be juice, the
environment becomes strongly acidic,
which makes many of the compounds in
beets less stable. A study from the
Harvard School of Public Health noted
that a strongly acidic environment can
reduce the bioavailability of certain
forms of nitrates, making it harder for
the body to convert them into nitric
oxide. In younger people, this reduction
may not matter much. But in older adults
whose absorption is already lower, the
effect becomes more noticeable. Beyond
the nitrate issue, lemon directly
affects the digestive system. After age
60, the stomach loses about 30 to 40% of
its ability to buffer acid. This makes
the stomach lining more sensitive to
strongly acidic foods. When beet juice,
which already gently stimulates bowel
movement, is combined with lemon, many
people experience burning in the
stomach, pain or pressure in the upper
abdomen, or persistent acid reflux. A
small survey at Mayo Clinic on patients
with reflux found that more than 60%
reacted strongly when they consumed
acidic juices together with nitraterich
foods. Another issue that many people
overlook is that lemon can cause mild
lightadedness in older adults. When acid
stimulates the stomach, the body
responds by increasing stomach
secretions, which can sometimes lead to
a temporary drop in blood sugar or small
shifts in blood pressure. If this
happens together with beet juice, which
already widens blood vessels, the
combined effect can make some people
feel weak, shaky, or as if their vision
darkens when they stand up. This does
not mean you must remove lemon from your

to safety is moderation. If you enjoy a
slight sour taste, use only a few drops

you have acid reflux, ulcers, or a
sensitive stomach, it is best to skip

juice. The most important thing for
older adults to remember is this.
Beetroot works best in a neutral or
slightly alkaline environment. The more
you interfere with strong acids, the
more the benefits go down and the risks
go up. Food number four, carrots.
Hook. Many people think that combining
one root vegetable with another root
vegetable must be even better and more
nutritious. But with beetroot and
carrots, this seemingly harmless
combination can silently burden the
kidneys, blood sugar, and circulation of
people over 60. Carrots are famous for
being rich in beta carotene, vitamin A,
and antioxidants. But what most people
do not know is that carrots also belong
to a group of foods with medium to high
oxalate levels. When they are juiced,
meaning most of the fiber is removed,
oxalates are absorbed much more strongly
than when they are eaten whole.
Beetroot, meanwhile, is in the very high
oxalate group. This combination creates
a double oxalate dose that forces the
kidneys of older adults to work much
harder. Studies published in the Journal
of Urology show that people over 60 have
almost 50% less ability to excrete
oxalates through the kidneys compared

with the same amount of oxalates, older

component of more than 70% of kidney

kidney stones, lower back pain, burning
when urinating, or pain while passing
urine. Another clinical survey from the

high oxalate juices, especially beet and

over 60. It is not just oxalates. Carrot
juice also releases a fairly high amount

glucose and fructose enter the
bloodstream much faster, causing a sharp

related insulin resistance, the carrot

shows that carrot juice raises blood

videos.title: Beetroot Juice on an Empty Stomach: Benefits Explained!

transcript.fullText: Every day, your heart does its silent work, pumping life through nearly 60,000 miles of blood vessels. But what if something as simple as a glass of beetroot juice could make that journey smoother, faster, and stronger? Welcome back to the Mind Hunters. Today, we're uncovering what happens when you drink beetroot juice on an empty stomach, and why scientists call it a natural nitric oxide booster. So, let's get started. Beetroots are rich in dietary nitrates, natural compounds that our body converts into nitric oxide, a molecule vital for life itself. Nitric oxide tells your blood vessels to relax and widen, allowing blood to flow more freely. This means your blood pressure drops, your heart pumps with less strain, and oxygen reaches your muscles and brain more efficiently. In scientific terms, this process is called vasoddilation, and it's one of nature's most elegant designs. Studies have shown that drinking beetroot juice can lower systolic blood pressure within hours, improving cardiovascular performance naturally. But why should you drink beetroot juice before breakfast? It's because when consumed before breakfast, beetroot juice works with maximum efficiency. An empty stomach allows nitrates to absorb faster, creating a quicker and stronger nitric oxide surge in the bloodstream. Within 30 to 45 minutes, blood vessels begin to open, circulation improves, and cells receive a fresh wave of oxygen and nutrients. Drinking beetroot juice has a chain reaction on health. The benefits ripple through the body. Your heart functions more efficiently. Your brain receives an enhanced oxygen supply, supporting focus and memory. Your muscles recover faster and perform better. Athletes often use beetroot juice as a natural preworkout enhancer, but its impact extends to everyday life, from lowering hypertension to protecting arteries. Beetroot's deep red pigment called betanin also acts as a powerful antioxidant, helping neutralize inflammation and supporting liver detoxification. Now let's discuss the right way to drink it. Science suggests moderation. A serving of 150 to 250 ml of fresh beetroot juice is sufficient. Drink it 30 minutes before breakfast for optimal absorption. videos.title: Drink Beet Juice, But Never Mix It With This (It Can Be Fatal!)

transcript.fullText: Did you know that a simple mistake in your kitchen can turn one of the healthiest foods in the world into something potentially dangerous? Every day, thousands of people wake up with the best of intentions and prepare their glass of beet juice, believing they are nourishing their bodies, strengthening their blood, and protecting their hearts. But what many don't know is that certain seemingly harmless combinations can trigger silent reactions in the body, overloading vital organs such as the liver and kidneys, altering blood pressure in dangerous ways, and even completely canceling out the benefits we seek. The scariest part is that this mistake happens daily in thousands of homes, hidden behind popular recipes that appear perfectly healthy. If you're thinking that adding a citrus fruit or a bit of honey is enough to improve the flavor, you need to hear what I am about to tell you. Because the truth is that beets, powerful as they are, require respect, balance, and understanding. And when we ignore the body's natural rules, we pay a high price, often without realizing it. Beets are a powerful root cultivated for millennia for their ability to restore vitality. Their deep red color reveals the richness of bioactive compounds they contain. The natural nitrates present in them convert into nitric oxide in the body, dilating blood vessels and improving circulation, acting like a natural remedy for high blood pressure and chronic fatigue. Beet juice also strengthens the liver through betain, which helps eliminate accumulated toxins and restore the body's balance. Additionally, it stimulates the production of red blood cells, improves cellular oxygenation, and fights aging with its antioxidants. Those who consume it regularly report more energy, mental clarity, and improved appearance. For people who exercise, it increases endurance, and speeds up muscle recovery, offering the body the natural fuel nature designed for us. But here's the problem. Many people, excited by so many benefits, fall into common traps. They believe that if beets are good, mixing them with other healthy ingredients will boost the results even more. And that's exactly where the danger lies. When you combine substances that react poorly together, the outcome can be the opposite of what you want. Instead of nourishing, you overload. Instead of healing, you inflame. Instead of strengthening, you weaken. In this video, we share seven beet juice combinations that may compromise your health and the best practices for consuming this nutritious juice. Don't miss combination number six, which deserves special attention. One, mixing beet juice with lemon. Let's start by talking about one of the most popular combination. Many people love adding lemon to beet juice, believing that the vitamin C in lemon will boost nutrient absorption. But what actually happens is quite different. Lemon is extremely acidic and when you mix it with raw beetroot, it drastically alters the pH of the drink. This irritates the stomach, especially in people who already have digestive sensitivity, gastritis, or acid reflux. Additionally, the excess acidity interferes with the absorption of the iron found in beets, cancelling one of the main benefits we seek when consuming this root. Sensitive individuals may experience heartburn, nausea, and even dizziness after drinking this combination. Lemon should be used with extreme moderation, just a few drops for freshness, never squeezed in large amounts. Two, mixing beet juice with honey or sugar. This is perhaps the most common mistake and one of the most harmful. Many people don't enjoy the earthy taste of beets and try to mask it with refined sugar or honey. The problem is that refined sugar completely blocks the action of nitrates, turning the juice into a glycemic bomb with no real benefits. Honey, though natural, intensifies the insulin response and causes blood sugar spikes. In people with diabetes, this habit can accelerate silent inflammatory processes and increase the risk of fat buildup in the liver. The secret is learning to appreciate the natural flavor of beetroot, which is mildly sweet and balanced. If you really need to soften the taste, use green apple or cucumber ingredients that add freshness without compromising the benefits. Three, mixing beet juice with orange. Now, let's talk about something even more dangerous. Have you ever thought about mixing beetroot with orange? Many people do this in search of a more pleasant, refreshing flavor. But this combination can cause intestinal fermentation, especially in those with sensitive stomachs or digestive issues. The acidity of the orange combined with the natural sugars in beetroot, creates a perfect environment for gas production and abdominal bloating. In addition, excess vitamin C alters the absorption of nitrates, the very compounds responsible for improving circulation and regulating blood pressure. In older adults or people with gastritis, the result may be immediate discomfort, heartburn, and malaise. It is much better to consume each juice separately at different times of the day, respecting the body's natural rhythm. Four, mixing beetroot with vinegar. Mixing beetroot with vinegar or any highly acidic drink such as fermented juices destroys part of the nitrates responsible for improving circulation. The acetic acid in vinegar can irritate the stomach and cause nausea, sickness, and burning sensations. For people with low blood pressure, the effect is even worse, potentially leading to dizziness and weakness. For those seeking detoxification, this combination is completely counterproductive. Instead of purifying the blood, it increases acidity and makes the liver's job harder. Five, mixing beetroot with milk and other dairy products. Some people also try to mix beetroot with milk to create a creamier smoothie-like texture. But this is an especially problematic combination. The acids present in beetroot react with the proteins in milk, forming compounds that hinder calcium absorption and cause fermentation in the stomach. The result is gas, bloating, and reflux. Milk should be consumed on its own. And if you want a smoother texture in your juice, choose coconut water or cucumber. Both hydrate and help balance the body's pH without causing unwanted reactions. Six, mixing beet juice with spinach. Both beetroot and spinach are foods rich in oxylic acid oxalates. When consumed together regularly, especially in concentrated juices, they can significantly raise oxalate levels in the body. Main risks. Kidney stones. Oxalates bind to calcium and can form calcium oxalate crystals, the most common cause of kidney stones. Reduced mineral absorption. Oxalates attach to important minerals such as calcium, iron, and magnesium, preventing your body from absorbing them properly. Problems in predisposed individuals. Anyone with a history of kidney stones, kidney issues, or poor mineral absorption should be especially careful. Although both foods are nutritious on their own, consuming them together frequently in concentrated juices is not recommended. If you enjoy both, have them on alternating days or choose other leafy greens with lower oxalate content, such as kale or mint, to mix with beetroot. Seven, mixing beetroot with carrots. Another very common mixture is beetroot with carrot. It sounds perfect, doesn't it? two powerful roots full of vitamins and minerals. But when consumed together in excess, they create a silent problem. Both beets and carrots are rich in oxalates, compounds that in high concentrations accumulate in the kidneys and may contribute to the formation of kidney stones. On top of that, both have a high content of natural sugars, which can quickly raise blood glucose levels, especially in people with insulin resistance or pre-diabetes. The body feels the impact of this overload with symptoms such as fatigue, bloating, and even lower back pain. The ideal approach is to alternate the consumption of these two roots, drinking beet juice one day and carrot juice the next, allowing the body to process the nutrients safely. Now that you know what to avoid, let's talk about how to consume beet juice the right way, making the most of every drop of its healing power. The first step is respecting the dose. One medium beetroot per day is enough to provide all the benefits without overloading the kidneys or liver. Many people believe that more is better, but with nitroarage foods, too much can have the opposite effect. The body responds best when it receives nutrients in small, consistent daily amounts with regularity. The juice should be consumed pure without artificial sweeteners or refined sugar. The purity of the drink is what makes it a true natural remedy. When you get used to the real flavor of foods, the body responds better because it recognizes what is natural and reacts with more energy and vitality. The best time to drink the juice is on a light, empty stomach, right after waking up or before lunch when the body is more receptive to nutrients. This way, mineral absorption is more efficient and the effects on circulation and energy become more noticeable. Avoid drinking it after heavy meals as slow digestion can compromise its action. A small glass taken calmly and mindfully is already enough to activate the metabolism and prepare the body for the day. Another essential point is freshness. The nutrients in beetroot begin to oxidize just minutes after being extracted. For that reason, it's best to prepare and consume the juice on the spot rather than storing it in the refrigerator for long periods. The fresher it is, the more active the nitric oxide will be. The compound responsible for improving blood flow and oxygenating the brain. In addition, the flavor is more intense and pleasant. Leaving the juice stored can reduce its benefits and even lead to natural fermentation. Beetroot is without a doubt one of the most powerful gifts nature has given us. When consumed the right way, it becomes a silent ally working in every cell to restore health from the inside out. So, the next time you prepare your beet juice, remember everything you learned today. If this content made sense to you and opened your eyes to something you were unknowingly doing wrong, give this video a like right now. This simple gesture shows me that you're with me on this journey of health and knowledge. And if you know someone who drinks beet juice every day, share this video with them. Comment below and tell me which combination you used to make and will stop making from today on. And if you're not yet subscribed to the channel, subscribe now and activate the notification bell to receive more content like this. We'll see you in the next video. And remember, your health begins with the choices you make every day. [Music]

so how can you make beetroot and ginger juice well it's quick and easy though you will need a juicer or a blender for the job start by washing and scrubbing your beets and ginger peel and cut the transcript.fullText: Let me ask you something, and I want you to think about this carefully. What if something you believe is helping your body every morning is quietly working against it? I've seen this happen more often than you might expect. Many men wake up feeling proud of their routine. They prepare a fresh glass of beet juice, rich in color, natural, powerful. They believe they're supporting their heart, improving circulation, boosting energy for the entire day. And they're not wrong. Beetroot is powerful. In fact, it's one of the most effective natural foods for supporting blood flow, opening blood vessels, and improving endurance. But here's the part almost no one explains clearly. When something is powerful, it also needs to be used correctly. Because just one small mistake, one harmless addition can completely change how your body responds to it. I'm Dr. Danielle Jones and for over 30 years I've worked with men dealing with circulation issues, energy loss, and metabolic changes as they age. And I can tell you this honestly, it's not the lack of healthy habits that causes problems. It's the small combinations, the ones that seem healthy on the surface, that quietly create stress inside the body. In today's video, I'm going to walk you through some of the most common beet juice combinations that may be doing more harm than good, including one that can place real strain on your kidneys and digestion, especially after 50. But more importantly, I'll show you how to use beet juice the right way so your body actually benefits from what it's capable of doing. Before we go further, I'd like to hear from you. Which of these sounds most familiar? You drink healthy juices but still feel low energy. You experience bloating or discomfort after certain drinks. Or you're not sure if what you're drinking is actually helping. Type one, two, or three in the comments. I read everyone and I'll help guide you toward what may help you most. Now, let's look at what's actually happening inside your body when you drink beet juice because this is where the real story begins. Beetroot is rich in natural compounds called nitrates. When you consume them, your body converts these nitrates into nitric oxide, a molecule that plays a critical role in your health. Nitric oxide helps your blood vessels relax and expand. This improves circulation, supports healthy blood pressure, and allows oxygen and nutrients to move more efficiently through your body. That's why many men notice better energy, improved endurance, and sometimes even stronger physical responsiveness. But here's what most people don't realize. This entire process from nitrate to nitric oxide is very sensitive. It depends on your digestive environment, your oral bacteria, the balance of acids and compounds in your stomach. And when that balance is disrupted, the process becomes weaker or stops working altogether. Let's take one example, oxalates. Beetroot naturally contains oxalates, plant compounds that are harmless in moderate amounts. But when combined with other high oxalate foods, the total load can increase quickly. Inside your body, oxalates bind with minerals like calcium and form crystals. Over time, these crystals can accumulate, especially in the kidneys. That's why certain combinations can increase the risk of kidney strain, particularly in men over 50 or those with reduced kidney function. Another factor is blood sugar response. When you juice vegetables, you remove most of the fiber. Fiber normally slows down how sugar enters your bloodstream. Without it, natural sugars are absorbed much faster. This can lead to rapid spikes in blood sugar, followed by energy crashes and increased stress on metabolic systems. For men with insulin resistance or slower metabolism, this effect becomes even more noticeable. There's also the issue of acidity. Some ingredients, especially acidic ones, can interfere with how beetroot compounds behave in your body. Too much acidity can irritate the stomach lining, disrupt digestion, and reduce the stability of key nutrients. In some cases, it can even interfere with how well your body absorbs important elements like plant-based iron. And finally, there's something many people overlook. Digestive compatibility. Not all healthy foods work well together. Each food requires a slightly different environment to be digested properly. When incompatible foods are combined, especially in concentrated liquid form like juice, the digestive system can struggle.
